Safety regulations require that environmental monitoring continue for two days after chemical treatment finishes before inspection can begin. At the same time, user training can start three days before the software configuration task is fully complete because only the final reporting module remains. Which scheduling approach should the project manager apply?

While developing the schedule, a project manager identifies two special relationships. Inspection must begin only after a mandatory waiting period following treatment, and end-user training may begin shortly before system configuration fully finishes because the remaining work does not affect the training content. What is the best way to model these relationships?

correct.text: Apply a lag between treatment and inspection, and a lead between configuration and training
correct.why: A lag models the required waiting period after predecessor completion, while a lead allows the successor to start before the predecessor fully finishes.

distractors.text: Add contingency reserve to the inspection activity and split the training activity into two separate work packages
distractors.trap: Uses reserve and decomposition instead of correctly modeling dependency logic.

distractors.text: Convert both relationships to finish-to-start dependencies with zero offset to keep the network simple
distractors.trap: Ignores real timing offsets and creates an inaccurate schedule model.

distractors.text: Use leads for both relationships because both activities are related by timing adjustments
distractors.trap: Confuses lag and lead by treating all offsets as acceleration.

distractors.text: Use lags for both relationships because each successor depends on predecessor progress
distractors.trap: Confuses waiting periods with overlaps that allow earlier successor start.

distractors.text: Record both situations as assumptions and leave the task relationships unchanged until execution begins
distractors.trap: Avoids schedule modeling even though the sequencing information is already known.

explanation: Leads and lags refine activity relationships by representing real timing offsets in the schedule network. A mandatory waiting period after a predecessor is a lag, while starting successor work before predecessor completion is a lead. Modeling both correctly improves forecast accuracy and avoids hidden schedule risk.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 6.3 Sequence Activities
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Develop Schedule
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Performance Domain

prompts: A project has an immovable launch date driven by a contractual market window. Schedule analysis shows the project will finish three weeks late. Two work streams could potentially be overlapped, but one pair of activities involves complex interface testing with a high probability of rework if performed in parallel. Additional qualified engineers are available for a design package on the critical path and can reduce its duration with minimal coordination overhead. What should the project manager do first?

During schedule optimization, a project manager must recover three weeks without moving the committed launch date. Overlapping some activities would create substantial rework risk in one critical integration area, while adding skilled engineers to another critical-path design effort would shorten duration at manageable cost. Which action is best?

correct.text: Crash the critical-path design package by adding the available qualified engineers
correct.why: Crashing is appropriate when added resources can shorten critical-path work with acceptable cost and less risk than overlapping high-rework activities.

distractors.text: Fast-track the interface testing activities because overlapping work always shortens the schedule most quickly
distractors.trap: Assumes fast-tracking is always preferred and ignores the stated rework risk.

distractors.text: Reduce scope in the design package because schedule recovery should avoid any increase in resource cost
distractors.trap: Treats scope reduction as a routine schedule technique rather than a separate scope decision requiring approval.

distractors.text: Ask the team to work overtime across all delayed activities, whether critical or not
distractors.trap: Uses unfocused effort instead of targeting compression on critical-path work.

distractors.text: Revise the launch milestone date because schedule compression introduces too much uncertainty
distractors.trap: Accepts slippage without first applying feasible schedule optimization techniques.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the schedule immediately because the current forecast shows late completion
distractors.trap: Confuses forecasting a variance with obtaining approval to change the baseline.

explanation: The question tests not just knowledge of crashing and fast-tracking, but judgment in selecting the lower-risk technique for the situation. Fast-tracking can shorten schedule but often increases rework and coordination risk, especially in tightly coupled technical work. When qualified resources are available for critical-path activities, crashing is the more appropriate first action if it provides compression with manageable cost and risk.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Develop Schedule
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 6.6 Control Schedule
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Planning Performance Domain

prompts: A project manager is finalizing a detailed predictive schedule for a global implementation. The network logic is complete and durations have been estimated, but several activities assigned to a specialist team in another country are scheduled during dates when that team is unavailable because of local holidays and maintenance shutdowns. If the project manager ignores this constraint, the milestone forecast will appear earlier than is realistically achievable. What should the project manager do?

During schedule development, a project manager notices that assigned work for a specialized support team falls on periods when the team is unavailable due to local holidays and planned shutdowns. The current forecast shows an attractive completion date, but it is not executable as scheduled. What is the best next step?

correct.text: Incorporate the resource calendars into schedule development and update the forecasted milestone dates accordingly
correct.why: Resource calendars constrain when assigned resources can actually perform work, so they must be reflected in the schedule to produce a realistic forecast.

distractors.text: Keep the current milestone date and address the unavailable periods later through day-to-day team coordination
distractors.trap: Treats a planning input as an execution detail and preserves an unrealistic schedule.

distractors.text: Replace the unavailable specialists in the schedule with generic resources so the baseline can be approved on time
distractors.trap: Substitutes placeholder staffing for validated capacity without confirming equivalent capability.

distractors.text: Convert the affected activities into milestones until the team confirms availability
distractors.trap: Misuses milestones, which have zero duration and cannot represent executable work.

distractors.text: Approve the schedule baseline first and then submit a change request if the shutdown periods cause delay
distractors.trap: Baselines an unworkable schedule instead of validating it before approval.

distractors.text: Focus on the activity list and defer calendar constraints because they are part of resource management rather than schedule management
distractors.trap: Incorrectly separates schedule development from resource availability constraints.

explanation: A schedule is credible only if it reflects actual resource availability. Resource calendars are a key input to schedule development because they define when specific resources can work, directly affecting activity dates and milestone forecasts. Ignoring them can create an approved baseline that is impossible to execute.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Develop Schedule
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Section 9.2 Estimate Activity Resources
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Schedule Management

prompts: A software implementation project has an approved scope baseline and schedule baseline. A technical architect proposes replacing a core platform component with a newer product that offers long-term maintainability advantages. The architect says the deliverables will remain functionally equivalent, but the deployment design records and configuration documentation would need to be updated. Which action should the project manager take first?

On a project with approved baselines, the architecture team recommends swapping one approved component for another with similar business capability but different technical specifications. The team says only the technical records and item descriptions need to be updated. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Assess whether the substitution affects approved baselines or plan components, and if so route it through integrated change control before updating configuration records
correct.why: A configuration update does not bypass the need for formal change review when approved baselines or plan elements may be affected.

distractors.text: Direct the configuration manager to update the technical documentation immediately because the business capability is unchanged
distractors.trap: Confuses configuration status updates with authorization to alter approved project content.

distractors.text: Allow the architect to implement the substitution as a corrective action because it improves maintainability
distractors.trap: Treats a design enhancement as an operational correction that needs no formal review.

distractors.text: Reject the proposal outright because any change after baseline approval is prohibited once execution has begun
distractors.trap: Incorrectly assumes baselines are fixed permanently rather than controlled through formal change processes.

distractors.text: Update the risk register only and monitor the architecture decision during the next status meeting
distractors.trap: Under-responds by documenting risk without addressing possible baseline impacts.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to negotiate with the vendor first and revisit governance after pricing is confirmed
distractors.trap: Focuses on downstream commercial activity before determining whether a controlled change is required.

explanation: The key distinction is that configuration management tracks the characteristics and status of project items, while change control governs proposed modifications to approved baselines and plan components. When a component substitution may affect scope definition, schedule logic, cost, quality planning, or other approved elements, the project manager must evaluate impact and route it through integrated change control before implementing documentation updates.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.4 Create WBS
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Project Performance Domain: Measurement

prompts: A project is entering execution, and the sponsor asks why the project management plan includes several subsidiary management plans rather than one short summary document. The sponsor wants assurance that these planning components will support consistent decision making when tradeoffs arise. Which statement best explains the purpose of integrating these planning components?

Before authorizing execution, a sponsor questions the value of maintaining separate subsidiary management plans within the overall project management plan. The sponsor wants to know how this structure helps the project manager make coordinated decisions. What is the best explanation?

correct.text: They provide coordinated direction for how the project will be executed, monitored, and controlled so decisions in one area are evaluated for effects on other areas
correct.why: Subsidiary plans are integrated to support coherent governance and coordinated decision making across the project.

distractors.text: They exist primarily so each functional manager can control work independently without needing project-level alignment
distractors.trap: Misunderstands subsidiary plans as separate functional documents rather than integrated project guidance.

distractors.text: They are used mainly to replace the need for performance reporting during execution
distractors.trap: Confuses planning artifacts with monitoring outputs.

distractors.text: They are created only to satisfy audit needs and are rarely used once the baseline is approved
distractors.trap: Treats management plans as compliance paperwork rather than active decision tools.

distractors.text: They ensure that schedule and cost decisions always take precedence over quality and risk considerations
distractors.trap: Assumes a fixed priority hierarchy instead of integrated tradeoff analysis.

distractors.text: They allow team members to change local work methods freely as long as the final deliverable is still produced
distractors.trap: Mistakes plan structure for permission to bypass coordinated management controls.

explanation: Subsidiary management plans are not independent administrative artifacts. Together, they form the integrated project management plan that guides execution and control across knowledge areas. Their value is in enabling coordinated tradeoff decisions and maintaining alignment as the project progresses.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.2 Develop Project Management Plan
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Project Performance Domain: Planning
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group

subtopic: reconciling baseline inconsistency across plan components
prompts: A project manager is finalizing planning for a regulated product launch. The scope, schedule, and cost baselines are approved, but a compliance lead identifies a mandatory control activity that was included in the quality management plan and omitted from the schedule model and resource planning. The launch date is aggressive and the sponsor wants no delay. What should the project manager do?

Near the end of planning, a required regulatory control appears in one subsidiary plan but is missing from other planning components needed to execute it. Baselines have already been approved and the sponsor is pushing to start immediately. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Analyze the impact of incorporating the control activity across the affected plan components and process the needed updates through formal change control before execution
correct.why: Once baselines are approved, resolving a planning inconsistency that changes authorized work requires integrated impact analysis and controlled updates.

distractors.text: Add the control activity to the team task list informally so compliance is addressed without disturbing approved baselines
distractors.trap: Assumes important planned work can be inserted operationally without updating controlled project documents.

distractors.text: Proceed with execution as planned and treat the missing control activity as an issue only if an audit finding occurs
distractors.trap: Defers known planning misalignment until failure materializes.

distractors.text: Remove the control from the quality management plan to keep the approved launch date intact
distractors.trap: Eliminates required work to preserve schedule rather than integrating and governing the change properly.

distractors.text: Ask the compliance lead to own the activity outside the project because regulatory tasks do not belong in the schedule baseline
distractors.trap: Incorrectly separates compliance work from integrated project planning.

distractors.text: Update only the resource management plan because the main problem is staffing, not planning integration
distractors.trap: Addresses one affected area while ignoring the need to reconcile all impacted baselines and plans.

explanation: The problem is not merely a missing task but a lack of integration among approved planning components. Because the omitted regulatory activity affects how work will be performed and likely impacts schedule, resources, and possibly cost, the project manager must assess impacts across the plan and route updates through formal change control. This preserves governance and keeps the project management plan internally consistent.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.2 Develop Project Management Plan
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11.2 Identify Risks

subtopic: Risk allocation in contractual terms and conditions
prompts: A project manager is finalizing an agreement with a vendor for a custom component that must interface with a regulated product. During negotiations, the vendor proposes broad limitation-of-liability language that would cap all damages, including those caused by noncompliance with regulatory requirements. The legal team says the clause is common in the market, but the sponsor is concerned about downstream exposure. What should the project manager do next?

While negotiating a supplier agreement for a regulated product component, a seller includes a standard clause that limits liability even for regulatory noncompliance. The clause is commercially common, but accepting it could leave the buyer exposed to major consequences later. What is the BEST action for the project manager?

correct.text: Escalate the clause for cross-functional review and negotiate revised terms that align liability allocation with the project's compliance risk exposure
correct.why: Contract terms should allocate risk deliberately, and regulatory exposure should not be accepted through standard language without targeted negotiation.

distractors.text: Accept the clause because standard market language is usually considered fair and enforceable
distractors.trap: Assumes common market practice automatically makes a term appropriate for the project's risk profile.

distractors.text: Reject the seller immediately and restart procurement to avoid any legal ambiguity
distractors.trap: Treats a negotiable contractual issue as requiring vendor replacement rather than managed negotiation.

distractors.text: Move the risk into the risk register after contract signature and rely on risk responses if noncompliance occurs
distractors.trap: Confuses documenting risk with transferring or controlling it through the agreement.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to approve the clause because business ownership of regulatory consequences belongs outside the project
distractors.trap: Improperly shifts the project manager's duty to coordinate appropriate agreement terms.

distractors.text: Convert the procurement to time and materials so the liability clause becomes less important
distractors.trap: Misunderstands contract type as a substitute for negotiating critical legal protections.

explanation: The issue is not whether the clause is common, but whether it aligns with the project's compliance and business risk exposure. In agreement negotiations, the project manager coordinates with legal and business stakeholders to ensure terms and conditions support project objectives and do not leave material risks inadequately addressed. The best response is to negotiate risk allocation intentionally rather than accept boilerplate language.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Conduct Procurements
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Control Procurements
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Executing Process Group, Conduct Procurements

subtopic: Balancing relationship management with enforceable remedies
prompts: A buyer and seller are negotiating a major services agreement. The seller's account manager insists that any schedule slippage caused by the seller should be handled informally through the relationship rather than through explicit contract language, because formal remedies could damage trust. The project manager wants a collaborative relationship but also needs enforceability. What should the project manager do?

During contract negotiations, a preferred supplier argues that schedule delays should be addressed through partnership discussions instead of written remedies in the agreement. The project manager wants to preserve a positive relationship without weakening control. What is the BEST response?

correct.text: Include clear performance, escalation, and remedy clauses in the agreement, framing them as governance that supports a transparent working relationship
correct.why: A constructive relationship is strengthened by clear contractual governance rather than by avoiding formal accountability.

distractors.text: Rely on a memorandum of understanding outside the contract so the legal agreement itself remains simple
distractors.trap: Assumes informal side documents provide the same control and enforceability as negotiated contract terms.

distractors.text: Remove the schedule remedies from the contract entirely to demonstrate good faith and promote collaboration
distractors.trap: Equates collaboration with avoiding explicit accountability.

distractors.text: Add aggressive financial penalties for every missed milestone to ensure the seller remains motivated
distractors.trap: Overcorrects toward punitive terms instead of balanced and workable governance.

distractors.text: Defer the issue until after award because remedies can be adjusted once the relationship is established
distractors.trap: Misunderstands that material commercial terms should be settled before agreement execution.

explanation: PMI-aligned procurement practice emphasizes both relationship management and clear agreement terms. The project manager should not trade enforceability for informality; instead, the agreement should define how performance issues are handled while preserving a professional and collaborative tone. Clear governance reduces ambiguity and protects both parties.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Conduct Procurements
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Control Procurements
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Executing Process Group, Conduct Procurements

subtopic: Handling uncertain buyer-furnished information in agreements
prompts: A project manager is negotiating a subcontract for specialized engineering work. The seller proposes language stating that all design assumptions provided by the buyer are deemed complete and accurate, and that any resulting rework will require equitable adjustment. The technical lead says several assumptions are still being validated. Which two actions should the project manager take before signing?

Before executing an engineering subcontract, a seller asks the buyer to accept contract language that treats current buyer-furnished design assumptions as final and compensable if they change. The project team knows some of those assumptions remain uncertain. What two actions should the project manager take first?

correct.text: Negotiate explicit wording that identifies provisional assumptions and defines how changes to those assumptions will be handled
correct.why: Uncertain inputs should be surfaced in the agreement so that adjustment mechanisms are clear and disputes are reduced.

correct.text: Align internal technical stakeholders on which assumptions are validated, which are provisional, and what level of contractual commitment is acceptable
correct.why: The project manager needs internal alignment before accepting legal language that shifts commercial consequences.

distractors.text: Sign the agreement now because all technical uncertainty can be managed through integrated change control later
distractors.trap: Confuses post-award change management with pre-award allocation of contractual responsibility.

distractors.text: Tell the seller to proceed under a letter of intent so design can start while assumptions remain undefined
distractors.trap: Uses early authorization to bypass needed clarity in commercial terms.

distractors.text: Transfer ownership of all assumptions to the seller because the seller is the technical expert
distractors.trap: Assumes expertise automatically justifies shifting all input risk to the supplier.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to remove references to assumptions entirely so the agreement stays high level
distractors.trap: Mistakenly treats ambiguity as risk reduction.

distractors.text: Replace the subcontract with a cost reimbursable agreement so assumption quality no longer matters
distractors.trap: Assumes contract form eliminates the need to define uncertain inputs and responsibilities.

explanation: When buyer-furnished information is uncertain, the project manager should make that uncertainty visible in the agreement rather than allow hidden assumptions to drive future claims. The best approach is to align internal stakeholders on acceptable risk and negotiate language that distinguishes validated information from provisional information, with a defined mechanism for handling changes. That reduces ambiguity and supports fair administration of the agreement.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Conduct Procurements
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Risk Management, Plan Risk Responses
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Plan Procurement Management

prompts: A project manager is negotiating with a sole-source supplier for a long-lead item. The supplier refuses to provide transparency into subcontractor dependencies and says that delivery commitments should exclude delays caused anywhere in its lower-tier supply chain. The project schedule has almost no float, and the organization would have limited visibility if a disruption occurred. What should the project manager do?

During negotiations with a sole-source vendor for a critical long-lead item, the seller wants delivery obligations carved back so lower-tier supplier delays are largely outside its responsibility. Because the project has minimal schedule contingency, this could create major exposure. What is the BEST action for the project manager?

correct.text: Negotiate reporting, notification, and escalation duties for lower-tier supply risks, tying delivery accountability to defined exceptions rather than broad exclusions
correct.why: The project needs visibility and bounded accountability so supply-chain risk is manageable rather than contractually invisible.

distractors.text: Accept the broad exclusion because sole-source suppliers typically cannot control their lower-tier supplier performance
distractors.trap: Assumes limited control justifies broad removal of accountability and visibility.

distractors.text: Instruct the team to increase schedule reserves after signing instead of challenging the supplier's language
distractors.trap: Uses internal contingency as a substitute for negotiating better agreement terms.

distractors.text: Convert the requirement into multiple smaller scheduled deliveries without changing the contract's accountability language
distractors.trap: Changes delivery structure without addressing the core accountability gap.

distractors.text: Have the sponsor manage the supplier relationship personally so the contract can remain less detailed
distractors.trap: Relies on executive influence instead of formal agreement controls.

distractors.text: Focus only on technical specifications because supply-chain management belongs entirely to the seller
distractors.trap: Separates procurement performance obligations from underlying delivery risk drivers.

explanation: For critical procurements, the agreement should not leave major upstream risks opaque. The project manager should negotiate targeted visibility, notification, and escalation mechanisms so emerging lower-tier disruptions are known early, while defining reasonable exceptions instead of accepting blanket disclaimers. This supports realistic schedule management and effective control of external dependencies.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Conduct Procurements
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Control Procurements
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Executing Process Group, Conduct Procurements

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A company is launching a regulatory reporting platform. Core compliance rules are fixed by law and require formal verification before release, but user-facing analytics features will evolve based on feedback from pilot users during development. The sponsor asks the project manager to recommend the most appropriate delivery approach and governance model at project initiation. What should the project manager do?

A project will deliver a compliance engine with stable mandatory requirements and a customer dashboard whose features are expected to change after early demonstrations. The organization wants one overall approach selected before planning is completed. Which methodology choice is most appropriate for the project manager to recommend?

correct.text: Use a hybrid approach with predictive planning and control for the compliance component and adaptive delivery for the analytics component, with integrated governance at the project level
correct.why: This aligns method selection to differing uncertainty, change cadence, and compliance needs within distinct parts of the same project.

distractors.text: Use a fully predictive approach because any regulatory content means the entire project must follow sequential planning and change control
distractors.trap: Assumes one compliance-heavy component automatically dictates predictive delivery for all work.

distractors.text: Use a fully agile approach because user feedback is the best way to maximize value across both components
distractors.trap: Ignores the need for formal verification and stability in the regulated portion of the solution.

distractors.text: Use an iterative approach for all deliverables because repeated cycles are equivalent to tailoring methodology
distractors.trap: Confuses iteration technique with a complete methodology decision.

distractors.text: Delay methodology selection until execution begins so the team can observe actual stakeholder reactions
distractors.trap: Treats methodology choice as something that should wait, rather than being intentionally selected and tailored during planning.

distractors.text: Create separate projects with different life cycles so each team can work independently without shared governance
distractors.trap: Assumes differing methods require organizational separation instead of integrated tailoring within one project.

explanation: The best choice is a hybrid model because the work contains components with materially different levels of volatility and compliance rigor. PMI guidance supports tailoring the approach to the nature of the work rather than forcing one method across all deliverables. Project-level governance can still remain integrated while execution practices differ by component.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Tailoring
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Section 3 Agile, Hybrid, and Predictive Approaches

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
subtopic: incremental value delivery versus partial iterative rework
prompts: A product owner tells the project manager that stakeholders want to see working software every month, but they also insist that each monthly release contain only fully usable business capability. Several team members propose delivering partially completed features across multiple cycles so progress appears steady. Which practice should the project manager support?

On a digital product project, executives want frequent visible delivery while operational users say each release must provide usable value with no partially deployable feature fragments. The team is debating whether to spread feature completion across several cycles. What is the best recommendation from the project manager?

correct.text: Deliver the product incrementally so each release contains completed, usable capability that provides value
correct.why: Incremental delivery is intended to provide deployable portions of functionality rather than incomplete feature slices.

distractors.text: Deliver iteratively by revisiting the same unfinished features over multiple cycles until enough work accumulates for deployment
distractors.trap: Confuses iterative refinement with the need to release usable value each cycle.

distractors.text: Switch to a predictive life cycle so all features are integrated only at the end, reducing release complexity
distractors.trap: Assumes release management concerns justify abandoning frequent value delivery.

distractors.text: Use a hybrid model in which all requirements are baselined first and no capability is released until final acceptance
distractors.trap: Describes a restrictive control posture that does not address the stated need for monthly usable releases.

distractors.text: Maintain monthly demonstrations only, because showing progress is equivalent to delivering value
distractors.trap: Equates stakeholder visibility with actual operational value delivery.

distractors.text: Organize releases around technical components so architecture is completed before any business capability is finished
distractors.trap: Prioritizes internal technical completion over customer-usable increments.

explanation: Incremental delivery is the strongest fit because the requirement is for each release to contain usable business capability. Iterative work can still occur inside development, but the release strategy should center on completed increments of value. PMI distinguishes iterative refinement from incremental delivery, and they are not interchangeable.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Section 2 Agile Overview
Agile Practice Guide, Appendix X3 Key Terms

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is initiating a machine-learning product for a new market segment. The business problem is understood only at a high level, stakeholder preferences are likely to shift after prototypes are reviewed, and technical feasibility is still being explored. Senior leadership asks for a methodology recommendation that reflects the work environment rather than organizational habit. What should the project manager recommend?

An organization wants to build an innovative AI-based service in an area with unclear customer expectations, evolving solution options, and significant discovery work. Executives usually prefer detailed upfront planning, but they ask the project manager to choose the method that best fits the context. Which recommendation is most appropriate?

correct.text: Recommend an adaptive approach that uses short feedback cycles, experimentation, and progressive elaboration because both requirements and solution are uncertain
correct.why: High uncertainty in both problem and solution spaces favors an adaptive method with rapid learning and frequent reassessment.

distractors.text: Recommend a predictive approach with detailed upfront decomposition because executive oversight is strongest when plans are fixed early
distractors.trap: Assumes control preference should override the project's actual level of complexity and uncertainty.

distractors.text: Recommend a standard hybrid approach by splitting analysis and build phases, even though neither scope nor solution is stable
distractors.trap: Applies hybrid as a default compromise instead of selecting practices based on the dominant uncertainty profile.

distractors.text: Recommend a sequential prototype phase followed by a locked predictive execution phase before stakeholders have validated the direction
distractors.trap: Prematurely freezes the approach before enough learning has occurred.

distractors.text: Recommend no methodology until the first release because complexity frameworks are used only after delivery problems appear
distractors.trap: Misunderstands complexity assessment as reactive rather than a basis for initial tailoring.

distractors.text: Recommend the same methodology used on the last infrastructure project to preserve organizational consistency
distractors.trap: Substitutes precedent for context-sensitive tailoring.

explanation: When both requirements and the solution are uncertain, an adaptive approach is usually most appropriate because it enables learning through inspection and adaptation. Complexity-aware tailoring focuses on the nature of the work, not on habit or executive comfort with upfront planning. Predictive methods fit better where scope and solution can be defined with greater confidence.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Section 3 Agile, Hybrid, and Predictive Approaches
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Tailoring

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
subtopic: tailoring practices at workstream level within a common framework
prompts: A project manager is leading a multiyear modernization program. One workstream involves replacing stable back-office infrastructure with known technical specifications, while another explores customer self-service capabilities that will be shaped through ongoing experiments. The PMO requires one coherent planning framework across the program. Which action best reflects appropriate tailoring?

In a transformation program, the infrastructure stream has clear interfaces and low expected change, but the digital experience stream depends on discovery and frequent reprioritization. The PMO asks the project manager to define how planning and delivery practices should be structured across the program. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Tailor the program so the stable infrastructure workstream uses predictive controls and the exploratory digital workstream uses adaptive practices, joined by common decision points
correct.why: This matches methods to the characteristics of each workstream while preserving alignment and governance across the program.

distractors.text: Standardize on a single predictive method for the whole program so status reporting stays comparable across the workstreams
distractors.trap: Confuses consistency of reporting with appropriateness of delivery method.

distractors.text: Adopt one agile method program-wide on the assumption that a modernization effort will benefit from adaptive delivery in every stream
distractors.trap: Treats transformation as automatically requiring agile, regardless of stable and well-understood work.

distractors.text: Delegate governance to each workstream lead so planning, metrics, and approval thresholds are defined locally with no shared program layer
distractors.trap: Ignores the need for integrated program-level oversight and alignment.

distractors.text: Roll out one uniform hybrid template, using the same ceremonies, artifacts, and iteration cadence for both workstreams
distractors.trap: Mistakes standardization of practices for effective tailoring.

distractors.text: Default to the PMO's preferred methodology, treating organizational standardization as more important than fitting the method to each workstream's uncertainty
distractors.trap: Elevates policy preference above fit-for-purpose selection.

explanation: PMI emphasizes tailoring based on the nature of the work, which can vary across workstreams within the same initiative. The best response is not to force one uniform delivery model, but to use a coherent governance framework with workstream-specific methods. This preserves strategic alignment while allowing each stream to use practices suited to its predictability or uncertainty.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Tailoring
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
Agile Practice Guide, Section 3 Agile, Hybrid, and Predictive Approaches

prompts: A project manager is planning procurement for a large data migration. The team can describe the required business outcomes and mandatory interfaces, but several qualified sellers use different proprietary migration methods. Some internal experts want the solicitation to prescribe the exact sequence of conversion steps each seller must follow so proposals are easier to compare. What should the project manager do?

A buyer organization is preparing procurement documents for a complex migration effort. Subject matter experts want the request package to tell sellers exactly how to perform the work internally, even though multiple technical approaches could achieve the required result. What is the best action by the project manager?

correct.text: Build the procurement statement of work around required deliverables, performance needs, constraints, and interfaces, without unnecessary direction on the seller's internal methods
correct.why: A procurement statement of work should define what the buyer needs and the boundaries of the work without overprescribing how the seller performs it.

distractors.text: Prescribe a single detailed sequence of conversion steps that every bidder must follow so that the proposals become easier to compare side by side
distractors.trap: Assumes easier comparison is more important than allowing qualified sellers to propose effective technical approaches.

distractors.text: Issue only a request for information first and ask sellers to define the business problem before the buyer documents its own needs
distractors.trap: Confuses market research with the buyer's responsibility to define procurement requirements.

distractors.text: Shift all technical requirements into the contract terms and conditions and leave the statement of work description deliberately minimal
distractors.trap: Misunderstands the difference between legal clauses and the definition of the procured work.

distractors.text: Have the buyer's preferred seller draft the work package before the solicitation is released to the market
distractors.trap: Creates bias and undermines fair competition in procurement planning.

distractors.text: Convert the procurement to staff augmentation so the buyer can direct external specialists through its own exact conversion process
distractors.trap: Confuses procurement of outcomes and deliverables with procurement of individual labor capacity.

explanation: The buyer should define the needed outcomes, deliverables, limits, and interfaces clearly enough for sellers to prepare responsive proposals. PMI terminology supports using a procurement statement of work to communicate required results rather than unnecessarily dictating the seller's internal execution approach. Overprescription can reduce innovation, competition, and overall value.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, Plan Procurement Management
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Plan Procurement Management

prompts: A project manager must solicit pricing for 500 identical industrial sensors with fully defined specifications, delivery location, and required delivery date. The only meaningful differentiator is price because all qualified sellers can meet the specification. Which bid document should the project manager use?

A buyer needs to procure a standard commodity item with complete technical specifications already established. Since the purchase decision will be based primarily on price, what is the most appropriate procurement document to issue?

correct.text: Issue an RFQ to obtain price quotations from qualified sellers for the defined commodity purchase
correct.why: An RFQ is appropriate when the item is well defined and the selection emphasis is on price.

distractors.text: Issue an RFI because the buyer should first ask the market how the sensors should be specified
distractors.trap: Assumes the buyer lacks requirements even though the specifications are already complete.

distractors.text: Issue an RFP because every external purchase should be evaluated through a detailed proposal process
distractors.trap: Confuses commodity pricing procurement with solution-oriented proposal procurement.

distractors.text: Ask each seller to submit a procurement statement of work for the buyer to compare
distractors.trap: Reverses the buyer and seller roles in defining the needed work.

distractors.text: Start direct negotiations with one incumbent seller because identical items do not require formal solicitation
distractors.trap: Bypasses appropriate competitive procurement without justification.

explanation: When the requirement is standardized and selection is driven mainly by price, an RFQ is the most suitable solicitation document. PMI distinguishes RFQ from RFP and RFI based on how well the need is defined and whether the buyer is seeking quotations, proposals, or information. This is a planning decision about the procurement approach, not a contract administration issue.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, bid documents
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Plan Procurement Management

prompts: A project manager is procuring exploratory engineering support for a product redesign. The buyer can define objectives and reporting expectations, but the actual effort may vary significantly as technical findings emerge. The sponsor wants work to begin quickly, maintain flexibility, and cap financial exposure. Which contract type is most appropriate?

A buyer needs external expert services for work that cannot be fully detailed in advance. The organization wants to pay agreed labor rates and material costs, retain flexibility as scope evolves, and protect itself with a not-to-exceed limit. Which contract type should the project manager plan to use?

correct.text: Use a time and materials contract with a ceiling price
correct.why: This contract fits work that cannot be fully defined upfront while limiting the buyer's financial exposure through a cap.

distractors.text: Use a firm-fixed-price contract because the buyer should transfer all cost risk to the seller whenever possible
distractors.trap: Assumes cost risk can be transferred effectively even when the work is not well defined.

distractors.text: Use a cost plus fixed fee contract because reimbursable contracts always provide the best buyer control
distractors.trap: Misstates buyer control and ignores the rate-based commercial structure described in the scenario.

distractors.text: Use a purchase order because the services are being bought externally
distractors.trap: Treats a complex uncertain service engagement as a simple routine acquisition.

distractors.text: Use a fixed-price incentive fee contract because incentives are required whenever technical uncertainty exists
distractors.trap: Assumes a stable target price can be established despite evolving work.

explanation: A time and materials contract is suitable when the buyer needs flexibility and cannot define the work in enough detail for a fixed-price arrangement. Adding a ceiling price helps protect the buyer from unlimited exposure. The key PMI logic here is matching contract type to uncertainty and allocation of risk during procurement planning.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, contracts
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Plan Procurement Management

prompts: A project manager is planning source selection for a critical cybersecurity service. One executive insists the lowest bidder should win, while the operations lead argues that past performance and response capability matter more because a failed implementation would interrupt regulated operations. What should the project manager do before issuing the solicitation?

A buyer organization is preparing to procure a high-risk operational service. Stakeholders disagree on whether price or operational capability should dominate the decision. Which action should the project manager take during procurement planning to support a fair and defensible seller selection?

correct.text: Define documented source selection criteria with appropriate weighting so proposals can be evaluated against project priorities and risk considerations
correct.why: Predefined weighted criteria create an objective and transparent basis for evaluating sellers according to best value.

distractors.text: State that price must determine the award because procurement should always maximize immediate cost savings
distractors.trap: Assumes lowest price is always the correct basis for award regardless of risk and performance needs.

distractors.text: Wait until proposals are received and then decide which criteria seem most useful for that seller pool
distractors.trap: Introduces bias by changing evaluation logic after seller responses are known.

distractors.text: Allow each stakeholder group to use its own evaluation criteria and reconcile differences after contract award
distractors.trap: Removes consistency and objectivity from the source selection process.

distractors.text: Ask the preferred seller to recommend the criteria that should be used in the buyer's evaluation model
distractors.trap: Compromises fairness by letting a potential seller shape the buyer's selection basis.

distractors.text: Skip formal evaluation criteria because the procurement team can rely on expert judgment during negotiations
distractors.trap: Confuses expert judgment with the need for defined and defensible source selection criteria.

explanation: The project manager should establish source selection criteria before solicitation so all sellers understand how proposals will be judged. Weighting helps the buyer balance price against capability, performance history, and operational risk in a transparent way. PMI expects procurement planning to support objective best-value decisions rather than ad hoc preferences.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Procurement Management, source selection criteria
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Plan Procurement Management

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is finalizing the approved project budget for a construction project. The sponsor asks whether management reserve should be included in the cost baseline used to measure cost performance. What should the project manager explain?
During budget approval, a sponsor asks if management reserve is part of the cost baseline for earned value measurements. What is the best response from the project manager?

correct.text: The cost baseline includes contingency reserves for identified risks but excludes management reserve, which is added to establish the project budget
correct.why: This matches PMI terminology. The cost baseline is the approved, time-phased budget used to measure and monitor cost performance. Contingency reserve is included within the baseline, while management reserve is outside the baseline and part of the overall project budget.

distractors.text: The cost baseline includes both contingency reserve and management reserve because both are part of planned project spending
distractors.trap: Confuses total project budget with cost baseline.

distractors.text: The cost baseline excludes both contingency reserve and management reserve because reserves are only added after execution begins
distractors.trap: Incorrectly assumes reserves are not planned during budgeting.

distractors.text: Management reserve is included in the cost baseline only when the project sponsor approves a change request
distractors.trap: Mixes reserve governance with baseline composition.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve is outside the cost baseline because it is used only for unknown risks
distractors.trap: Reverses the meaning of contingency reserve and unknown risk treatment.

explanation: PMI distinguishes clearly between the cost baseline and the project budget. The cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used to assess cost performance and it includes contingency reserves for identified risks. Management reserve is held for unforeseen work that is within project scope but not included in the cost baseline. When management reserve is added to the cost baseline, the result is the total project budget.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Budget and Cost Baseline
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.3 Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.2 Estimate Costs

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A software project has several senior developers assigned to overlapping activities. The resource histogram shows that two future weeks exceed the available capacity of the development team. The delivery date cannot move, but some activities have float. Which scheduling technique should the project manager apply first?
A resource histogram reveals temporary over-allocation of key developers. The project finish date must remain unchanged, although noncritical activities have flexibility. What is the best action?

correct.text: Apply resource smoothing to adjust activities within available float while preserving the planned finish date
correct.why: Resource smoothing is used when the project end date must be maintained and adjustments can be made within float to reduce peaks in resource demand.

distractors.text: Apply resource leveling even if the critical path changes and the end date is extended
distractors.trap: Resource leveling may delay the schedule, which conflicts with the constraint that the finish date cannot move.

distractors.text: Increase management reserve to cover the over-allocation of developers
distractors.trap: Reserve funding does not resolve resource over-allocation.

distractors.text: Perform funding limit reconciliation to align labor demand with team capacity
distractors.trap: Funding limit reconciliation addresses expenditure constraints, not direct resource balancing.

distractors.text: Reclassify the affected work packages as analogous estimates and update the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Estimate type changes do not address resource over-allocation.

explanation: The key distinction is whether the schedule end date can change. Resource smoothing adjusts activities within free or total float so resource usage becomes more uniform without changing the critical path or planned completion date. Resource leveling is more aggressive and can alter the critical path and extend the schedule. Because the finish date is fixed and some activities have float, smoothing is the appropriate first response.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Develop Schedule
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Data Representation, Resource Histogram
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Resource Optimization

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager develops a time-phased budget and discovers that planned expenditures exceed the organization funding cap in the third quarter. The scope is unchanged and the sponsor wants the budget release profile to stay within quarterly limits. What should the project manager do?
While establishing the project budget, the project manager finds that the planned cost baseline exceeds the available funding limit for one period. Which action is most appropriate?

correct.text: Perform funding limit reconciliation by adjusting the timing of work and expenditures to align the baseline with funding constraints
correct.why: Funding limit reconciliation compares planned expenditures against funding limits and may require rescheduling work to produce a realistic and authorized baseline.

distractors.text: Add management reserve to the third quarter so the cost baseline matches the funding cap
distractors.trap: Management reserve does not solve period funding constraints and is not part of the cost baseline.

distractors.text: Convert the estimate to a rough order of magnitude estimate because definitive estimates are too detailed for funding approval
distractors.trap: Estimate class is unrelated to reconciling period-by-period funding limits.

distractors.text: Use resource leveling only, because funding issues are always caused by resource over-allocation
distractors.trap: Funding constraints and resource constraints are related but not identical.

distractors.text: Measure cost performance against earned value before changing the baseline
distractors.trap: This is premature because the issue arises during planning, before execution measurement.

explanation: Funding limit reconciliation is performed during budget development when the planned expenditures in the cost baseline exceed available funding for a given period. The project manager analyzes options such as shifting activities, procurement timing, or expenditure profiles so the time-phased baseline aligns with actual funding constraints. This ensures the approved baseline is realistic and implementable within organizational cash flow limits.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.3 Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Tool and Technique, Funding Limit Reconciliation
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Budget

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is in early initiation and executives want a quick cost estimate to evaluate whether to authorize charter development. Only high-level scope information is available. Several months later, after detailed scope definition and complete design, the team must update the estimate for procurement and baseline approval. Which pair of estimate types is most appropriate?

An organization needs an early screening estimate before detailed planning, then a much more accurate estimate after the scope and design are fully defined. Which estimates should be used in those two stages?

correct.text: Use a rough order of magnitude estimate during initiation and a definitive estimate after detailed planning and design
correct.why: ROM estimates support early feasibility and selection decisions when uncertainty is high, while definitive estimates are prepared later when scope is well defined and are suitable for final budgeting and procurement decisions.

distractors.text: Use a definitive estimate during initiation and an analogous estimate after detailed design
distractors.trap: Reverses the normal progression of estimate accuracy and maturity.

distractors.text: Use a parametric estimate during initiation and avoid a definitive estimate because it reduces flexibility
distractors.trap: Parametric may be possible in some contexts, but it does not replace the general need for a definitive estimate when details are known.

distractors.text: Use a bottom-up estimate during initiation and a rough order of magnitude estimate for baseline approval
distractors.trap: Bottom-up requires detailed scope, and ROM is not suitable for final baseline approval.

distractors.text: Use management reserve as the initial estimate and contingency reserve as the final estimate
distractors.trap: Confuses reserves with estimate types.

explanation: Estimate accuracy should increase as project information matures. In early initiation, only broad scope and assumptions are available, so a rough order of magnitude estimate is appropriate for feasibility and portfolio decisions. After planning and design are sufficiently developed, a definitive estimate provides the accuracy needed for procurement, budget approval, and cost baseline development. The question tests recognition of estimate maturity across the project life cycle.
references: Practice Standard for Project Estimating, Estimate Classification Concepts
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.2 Estimate Costs
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Estimating

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is reviewing planned work against team capacity and notices that several specialist roles are heavily overallocated during the next two months. Which project artifact is most appropriate to visualize this pattern and support a discussion about adjusting assignments?

During resource planning, the project manager needs a time-phased view showing how many hours of each specialist role are needed per period so overallocations can be identified. What should the project manager use?

correct.text: A resource histogram
correct.why: A resource histogram displays the amount of time or units that resources are scheduled over time, making periods of overallocation and underutilization visible.

distractors.text: A stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Confuses stakeholder analysis with time-phased resource usage data.

distractors.text: A risk register
distractors.trap: Assumes all staffing concerns should be managed primarily as risks rather than through resource planning artifacts.

distractors.text: A cost performance report
distractors.trap: Focuses on spending results rather than visualizing resource demand by period.

distractors.text: A procurement statement of work
distractors.trap: Applies to seller deliverables and acquisition scope, not internal resource loading patterns.

explanation: The best artifact is a resource histogram because it provides a visual, time-phased display of resource requirements and helps the project manager identify peaks, shortages, and opportunities to rebalance assignments. Other artifacts may contain useful project information, but they do not directly show demand for resource units over time in a way that supports staffing decisions.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.2 Estimate Activity Resources
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.2.3.1 Resource Requirements
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.2.3.2 Basis of Estimates

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
subtopic: Resource availability and schedule impact analysis
prompts: A project manager is planning a software release and learns that two key developers will be unavailable for three weeks due to mandatory operational support. The release date is fixed by regulation. What should the project manager do first?
On a compliance project with a fixed deadline, critical team members are partially unavailable during an upcoming period. What is the most appropriate first action for the project manager?

correct.text: Analyze how the three-week gap affects the critical path and resource plan, then weigh options such as resequencing or backfill with the team and sponsor
correct.why: The project manager should first analyze the effect of reduced availability on planned work and then determine viable responses through integrated planning and stakeholder engagement.

distractors.text: Immediately draw on management reserve to fund overtime for the remaining developers so the schedule is protected
distractors.trap: Treats management reserve as the first response and assumes overtime is automatically appropriate without analysis or approval.

distractors.text: Drop the planned quality assurance activities for this release so the fixed regulatory date can still be met
distractors.trap: Sacrifices required quality processes instead of managing constraints in an integrated way.

distractors.text: Record the availability change in the lessons learned register and keep the current assignments unchanged for now
distractors.trap: Focuses on documentation instead of addressing an active planning issue.

distractors.text: Escalate straight to the PMO and ask it to replace the two unavailable developers with new resources
distractors.trap: Jumps to escalation before evaluating the actual impact and available options.

explanation: When resource availability changes, the project manager should first determine how the change affects the schedule, planned work, and resource approach. That analysis supports informed decisions such as resequencing, alternative staffing, approved overtime, or scope tradeoffs. Immediate spending, escalation, or cutting quality activities without integrated impact analysis would be premature and inconsistent with disciplined project control.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.1 Plan Resource Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Schedule Management, section 6.5 Develop Schedule
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.6 Control Resources

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is developing the project budget for a construction project. Labor rates, material prices, and equipment rental costs are known for each work package. Which budgeting technique is most appropriate to build the overall project budget from this detailed information?
Detailed cost estimates have been completed for each work package, including labor, material, and equipment costs. How should the project manager most appropriately develop the total project budget?

correct.text: Aggregate the estimated costs of the work packages up through the levels of the WBS to establish the project budget
correct.why: Cost aggregation rolls up detailed activity or work package estimates through the WBS to develop control accounts and the total approved budget.

distractors.text: Base the total on analogous estimating from a similar past construction project rather than the current detailed figures
distractors.trap: Ignores the availability of detailed current estimates and relies on a less precise top-down method.

distractors.text: Derive the budget baseline directly from the approved schedule baseline for the project
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule planning with cost budgeting.

distractors.text: Build a resource histogram of staffing demand and read the total authorized budget from it
distractors.trap: Uses a staffing visualization tool instead of a budgeting technique.

distractors.text: Apply earned value formulas such as estimate at completion to forecast the initial project budget
distractors.trap: Uses performance control methods for execution rather than establishing the original budget.

explanation: When detailed estimates exist at the work package or activity level, the appropriate budgeting technique is cost aggregation. The project manager rolls those estimates up through the WBS to form control accounts and the full project budget. Analogous estimating is less precise, while earned value analysis is mainly used later to measure and forecast performance during project execution.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, section 7.3 Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, section 7.3.2.1 Cost Aggregation
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, section 7.2 Estimate Costs

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is in execution and actual costs are rising faster than planned because a critical material has increased in price. The scope has not changed. Which action best supports effective budget management by the project manager?

During project execution, procurement costs increase significantly due to market conditions, while approved scope remains unchanged. What should the project manager do next to manage the budget appropriately?

correct.text: Analyze the variance, determine the impact on the cost baseline and forecasts, and process a change request if needed
correct.why: Budget management requires understanding the cause and impact of the variance, updating forecasts, and using formal change control when the approved baseline must change.

distractors.text: Revise the cost baseline immediately and inform the team
distractors.trap: Assumes the project manager can change an approved baseline without formal change control.

distractors.text: Move funds from unrelated work packages without documentation
distractors.trap: Treats the budget as freely interchangeable and bypasses governance and traceability.

distractors.text: Ignore the variance until the next reporting cycle because market changes are external
distractors.trap: Assumes external causes do not require timely project control action.

distractors.text: Reduce project scope informally to offset the material increase
distractors.trap: Introduces unauthorized scope changes instead of using integrated change control.

explanation: Effective budget management during execution means the project manager monitors actual performance, analyzes cost variances, evaluates their effect on forecasts and the approved baseline, and submits a change request when required. A baseline cannot be changed informally, and undocumented fund shifts or scope reductions undermine governance, traceability, and stakeholder alignment.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, section 7.4 Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Integration Management, section 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, section 7.4.2.2 Data Analysis

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager is preparing the resource management plan for a large cross-functional project involving internal staff and contracted specialists. Which two components should be included to help clarify how resources will be acquired, assigned, and released?
For a complex project using both employees and external specialists, which two elements belong in the resource management plan to define how team and physical resources will be obtained and managed?

correct.text: Roles and responsibilities for project resources
correct.why: The resource management plan defines who does what, including authority, responsibilities, and required competencies.

correct.text: Resource acquisition and release criteria
correct.why: The plan describes how and when team and physical resources will be acquired and released so resource use is coordinated and controlled.

distractors.text: The full issue log with all current owner assignments
distractors.trap: Confuses an evolving execution artifact with planned resource management guidance.

distractors.text: Product acceptance criteria for each deliverable
distractors.trap: Places scope and quality content into the resource management plan.

distractors.text: Detailed seller invoices for contracted specialists
distractors.trap: Mistakes procurement transaction records for planning components.

distractors.text: The benefits management plan for the program
distractors.trap: Uses a business value governance artifact rather than a project resource planning element.

distractors.text: A complete list of all project risks and responses
distractors.trap: Substitutes the risk register for resource planning content.

explanation: The resource management plan explains how team and physical resources will be estimated, acquired, managed, and released. Core components include clear roles and responsibilities as well as resource acquisition and release criteria. By contrast, issue logs, acceptance criteria, invoices, benefits planning, and complete risk listings belong to other management artifacts and processes.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.1 Plan Resource Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.1.3.1 Resource Management Plan
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management, section 9.3 Acquire Resources

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project is nearing completion, and the customer informally states that the deliverable looks acceptable. What should the project manager do NEXT to complete closure properly?
At the end of a phase, a key stakeholder verbally confirms satisfaction with the output. What is the most appropriate next action by the project manager to support proper phase closure?

correct.text: Obtain formal documented acceptance of the deliverable from the authorized stakeholder
correct.why: Formal acceptance is required to confirm that scope has been completed and accepted according to agreed criteria before closure proceeds.

distractors.text: Release the project team immediately because the work is essentially complete
distractors.trap: Assumes verbal satisfaction is enough to trigger closure activities and resource release.

distractors.text: Archive all project records before confirming acceptance
distractors.trap: Reverses the proper order by performing administrative closure before formal acceptance.

distractors.text: Begin procurement closure for all contracts regardless of deliverable status
distractors.trap: Treats contract closure as the first step even though product acceptance should be confirmed first.

distractors.text: Transfer the deliverable to operations without additional approval
distractors.trap: Confuses handover with formal acceptance and skips governance requirements.

explanation: In PMI terminology, project or phase closure requires formal acceptance of completed deliverables by the appropriate authorized stakeholder, not merely verbal approval or general satisfaction. Once acceptance is documented, the project manager can proceed with transition, administrative closure, records archiving, and resource release in an orderly manner.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 4.7 Close Project or Phase
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 5.5 Validate Scope
Process Groups Practice Guide Closing Process Group

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project manager is closing a phase of an infrastructure project. Which action best ensures organizational learning is preserved for future work?
During phase closure, what should the project manager do to maximize reuse of experience on future projects?

correct.text: Facilitate a lessons learned review and store the resulting knowledge in organizational process assets
correct.why: Lessons learned should be captured, validated, and archived in a reusable repository so future projects can benefit from the experience.

distractors.text: Document only major failures because successes are already reflected in the final deliverable
distractors.trap: Misunderstands lessons learned as a defect log rather than balanced organizational knowledge.

distractors.text: Wait until the entire program ends so that all lessons can be collected at one time
distractors.trap: Ignores the value of capturing lessons at phase closure while information is current.

distractors.text: Send an informal email summary to the team and consider the knowledge captured
distractors.trap: Assumes ad hoc communication replaces structured capture and archiving.

distractors.text: Focus on performance reports because they automatically contain all lessons learned
distractors.trap: Confuses reporting data with reflective analysis and explicit knowledge capture.

explanation: PMI guidance emphasizes capturing lessons learned throughout the project and especially during phase or project closure. The project manager should conduct a structured review, identify what worked and what did not, and place that information into organizational process assets so that it becomes retrievable and useful beyond the immediate team.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 4.4 Manage Project Knowledge
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 4.7 Close Project or Phase
Process Groups Practice Guide Closing Process Group

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A software implementation project is moving from delivery into operations. Which TWO actions should the project manager take to support an effective transition?
A project deliverable has been accepted and will now be handed over to the operations team. Which TWO actions are most appropriate during closure and transition?

correct.text: Confirm that the transition or handover plan includes training, support needs, and operational readiness activities
correct.why: A complete transition plan ensures the receiving organization can sustain and use the deliverable effectively after project closure.

correct.text: Transfer required documentation, knowledge, and ownership responsibilities to the receiving team
correct.why: Handover is not just physical delivery of an output; it includes documentation, accountability, and knowledge transfer.

distractors.text: Keep project ownership in place until the next project starts to avoid accountability gaps
distractors.trap: Assumes ownership should remain with the project rather than transferring to operations after acceptance.

distractors.text: Delay transition until all project team members have been reassigned
distractors.trap: Makes resource logistics a prerequisite for operational handover.

distractors.text: Close the project immediately after acceptance because operations can determine transition details later
distractors.trap: Separates acceptance from transition planning and risks operational disruption.

distractors.text: Archive all records before sharing technical and support documentation with operations
distractors.trap: Places record storage ahead of essential handover activities.

explanation: A proper transition from project work to operations requires an intentional handover approach. PMI terminology supports ensuring readiness of the receiving organization, including training, support arrangements, documentation transfer, and clear ownership. Acceptance alone does not guarantee successful operational adoption; a structured transition plan closes that gap.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 4.7 Close Project or Phase
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Delivery and Measurement performance domains
Process Groups Practice Guide Closing Process Group

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/Phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project has completed all deliverables and received final acceptance. Several contractors are still engaged and some records remain scattered across team drives. What should the project manager do NEXT to complete closure properly?
After customer acceptance, a project manager discovers open procurement files and incomplete records retention. What is the best next step in closing the project?
