distractors.trap: Mistakes stakeholder dissatisfaction for a risk evaluation issue rather than a completion and quality assessment issue.

explanation: When a stakeholder questions whether the demonstrated product meets expectations, the first check is whether the increment satisfies the team's agreed completion standard. The Definition of Done establishes the quality and completeness conditions for work to be considered finished. Acceptance criteria are relevant at the story level, but the best artifact for assessing whether the demonstrated work is done is the DoD.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Definition of Done
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Performance Domain 'Delivery'
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Performance Domain 'Quality'

prompts: During development of the scope management plan, a stakeholder requests adding pilot technology to the project scope to support product marketing. What is the correct action for the project manager?
While preparing the scope management plan, a stakeholder asks to include pilot technology in the project scope to help market the product. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Include the stakeholder in the scope planning meetings
correct.why: Engaging the stakeholder in scope planning ensures the request is evaluated collaboratively before any scope decision is made.

distractors.text: Increase the project budget to include the cost of the pilot technology
distractors.trap: Assumes a requested addition should be funded before it is formally reviewed and approved as scope.

distractors.text: Review the scope baseline with the stakeholder
distractors.trap: Treats the baseline as the starting point before it has even been established during planning.

distractors.text: Use the project reserve for the pilot technology
distractors.trap: Misuses reserves to add new scope without proper planning and approval.

distractors.text: Direct the team to add the pilot technology to avoid stakeholder dissatisfaction
distractors.trap: Assumes stakeholder influence overrides formal scope planning and control.

explanation: Because the project is still developing the scope management plan, the appropriate action is to involve the stakeholder in scope planning discussions. That allows the proposed addition to be understood, evaluated, and incorporated through the proper planning process if justified. The other options prematurely commit budget or implementation before scope has been properly planned and agreed.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.1, Plan Scope Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.2, Collect Requirements
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Stakeholder Performance Domain

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
subtopic: resource availability in a matrix environment
prompts: During project execution, an experienced team member tells the project manager that they cannot participate in all scheduled project activities because of a conflicting operational schedule. What should the project manager do?
While the project is being executed, a key team member explains that operational duties conflict with several planned project activities. What is the best action for the project manager?

correct.text: Discuss the situation with the team member's Functional Manager to see if the schedule can be adjusted
correct.why: The functional manager typically has authority over the team member's operational commitments and can help resolve the resource conflict.

distractors.text: Encourage the team member to prioritize scheduled project activities
distractors.trap: Assumes the team member can independently override competing operational responsibilities.

distractors.text: Check with the Project Management Office or PMO
distractors.trap: Confuses governance support with direct authority over functional resource schedules.

distractors.text: Replace the team member with another available resource
distractors.trap: Moves too quickly to substitution without first trying to resolve the allocation conflict through the proper manager.

distractors.text: Reschedule all project activities around the team member's operational work
distractors.trap: Assumes the whole project should be adjusted before exploring a targeted resource solution.

explanation: In a matrix or shared-resource setting, the functional manager is the appropriate party to address conflicts between operational duties and project work. Discussing the issue there is the most effective way to seek a workable adjustment while respecting organizational authority. The other choices either bypass that authority or take premature action.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.3, Acquire Resources
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.5, Manage Team
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain and stakeholder collaboration

prompts: During initial project scheduling, an internal key stakeholder says they already committed to certain delivery dates with a vendor. Which dependency technique should the project manager use to ensure all prerequisite work is completed so those deliverables can meet the agreed dates?

While developing the schedule, the project manager learns that an internal stakeholder has already agreed with a vendor on delivery dates. What type of dependency should the project manager identify to confirm the required predecessor activities are fulfilled for those dates?

correct.text: Mandatory dependencies
correct.why: Mandatory dependencies identify legally, physically, or contractually required sequencing constraints that must be satisfied before deliverables can occur on the committed dates.

distractors.text: Finish-to-finish dependencies
distractors.trap: Confuses a relationship type between activities with the broader concept of identifying required dependencies that constrain scheduling.

distractors.text: Discretionary dependencies
distractors.trap: Assumes the dates are based on preferred sequencing rather than required prerequisites driven by firm commitments or constraints.

distractors.text: Internal dependencies
distractors.trap: Mistakenly classifies the issue by organizational source instead of by whether the dependency is required and unavoidable.

distractors.text: Start-to-start dependencies
distractors.trap: Focuses on a specific precedence relationship even though the question is asking for the dependency category that ensures prerequisites are fulfilled.

explanation: The scenario centers on committed delivery dates that require prerequisite work to occur in a required sequence. In PMI scheduling practice, mandatory dependencies are inherent or required by the nature of the work or external commitment and therefore must be reflected in the schedule. Relationship labels such as finish-to-finish or start-to-start describe activity logic, but they do not answer the question of which dependency category ensures required prerequisites are honored.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 6.3 Sequence Activities
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 6.3.2.2 Dependency Determination and Integration
Process Groups A Practice Guide Planning Process Group Schedule Planning

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: At the project kick-off meeting, the project manager announces that the project will use a hybrid delivery approach. Many team members are concerned because they are accustomed only to predictive projects. What should the project manager do?
A project begins with a hybrid approach, but several team members are uneasy because they have mostly worked in predictive environments. What is the best action for the project manager to take?

correct.text: Educate the team members on agile and the benefits associated with the use of agile principles.
correct.why: A hybrid environment requires shared understanding of how agile practices add value, so the project manager should build awareness and alignment rather than retreat from the selected approach.

distractors.text: Engage the project management office PMO since they identify the approach to delivering projects.
distractors.trap: Assumes the PMO always dictates delivery methodology instead of recognizing the need to help the team understand and work within the chosen approach.

distractors.text: Change to a predictive approach to be aligned with the skills of the team members.
distractors.trap: Treats team discomfort as a reason to abandon an appropriate methodology rather than coaching and enabling the team.

distractors.text: Delay project initiation until all team members gain prior agile project experience.
distractors.trap: Assumes experience must precede adoption instead of supporting the team through training and practical transition.

distractors.text: Allow each team member to choose whether to follow agile or predictive practices individually.
distractors.trap: Mistakes flexibility for lack of governance and ignores the need for a coherent delivery approach.

explanation: When a hybrid approach is selected, the project manager should help the team understand how the method works and why it is being used. PMI guidance emphasizes tailoring and supporting adoption through education, coaching, and shared understanding. Changing the approach solely because people are unfamiliar with it is usually weaker than building capability in the team.
references: Agile Practice Guide Section 3 Agile Hybrid and Predictive Life Cycles
Agile Practice Guide Section 4.2 Tailoring Considerations
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Performance Domain Tailoring

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: During which phase of a project do staffing levels typically reach their highest point?
In a typical project life cycle, when are staffing levels usually at their peak?

correct.text: Execution phase
correct.why: Staffing is usually highest during execution because most project work is being performed and resources are fully engaged in producing deliverables.

distractors.text: Planning phase
distractors.trap: Assumes detailed planning requires the same level of resource deployment as performing the actual work.

distractors.text: Closure phase
distractors.trap: Confuses final acceptance and transition activities with the peak workload of delivering project outputs.

distractors.text: Initiation phase
distractors.trap: Mistakes early authorization and high level definition for the labor intensive stage of the life cycle.

distractors.text: Monitoring and controlling phase
distractors.trap: Treats an ongoing management function as a distinct peak staffing stage instead of recognizing execution as the usual high point.

explanation: In the standard project life cycle, staffing and cost are generally low at the start, rise as work progresses, peak while the project is being executed, and decline toward closure. Execution is where the bulk of deliverable creation occurs, so it typically requires the greatest concentration of resources. Monitoring and controlling happens throughout the project, but it does not usually represent the highest staffing level.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Section 1.2.4 Project Life Cycle
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Figure 1-4 Cost and Staffing Levels Across the Project Life Cycle
Process Groups A Practice Guide Executing Process Group

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: Projects vary in their cash flow patterns, procurement timing, and liabilities across the life cycle, but some characteristics are typical on most projects regardless of type or complexity. Which statement best describes a typical project scenario?
Although projects differ in how funding and spending occur across phases, certain life cycle patterns are common to most projects. Which of the following is generally true?

correct.text: Staffing peaks during the execution phase of a project.
correct.why: Resource usage commonly rises as work is performed and typically reaches its highest level during execution.

distractors.text: The next phase of a project should never start until the deliverables for the previous phase are completely reviewed and approved.
distractors.trap: Assumes all projects must use a strict sequential phase-gate model and ignores overlaps such as fast tracking.

distractors.text: Changes in project scope during the initial phases of the project are very expensive.
distractors.trap: Reverses the common cost-of-change pattern, where changes are usually least expensive early in the project.

distractors.text: The influence of stakeholders is the same throughout the project.
distractors.trap: Ignores that stakeholder influence is typically highest early in the project and declines over time.

distractors.text: Risk is lowest at the beginning of the project and increases steadily until closure.
distractors.trap: Misunderstands the usual project risk trend, which is generally highest at the start and decreases as uncertainty is reduced.

explanation: A typical project pattern is that staffing and resource needs increase during execution when most deliverable work is being performed. By contrast, stakeholder influence is usually greater at the beginning, changes are generally less costly earlier rather than later, and phases may overlap depending on the life cycle. The distractors each reflect common misunderstandings about standard project life cycle trends.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.4 Project Life Cycle
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Figure 1-3 Characteristics of the Project Life Cycle

prompts: Expected monetary value, or EMV, is calculated using which equation?
Which formula correctly defines expected monetary value for project risk analysis?

correct.text: Multiplying the value of each possible outcome by the probability of occurrence and adding the products together
correct.why: EMV is the sum of each possible outcome weighted by its probability of occurring.

distractors.text: Value of each possible outcome multiplied by probability of non-occurrence
distractors.trap: Uses the wrong probability and also omits summing across multiple possible outcomes.

distractors.text: Value of each possible outcome multiplied by probability of occurrence
distractors.trap: Captures only part of the concept and misses the required addition of all weighted outcomes.

distractors.text: Multiplying the value of each possible outcome by the probability of non-occurrence and adding the products together
distractors.trap: Applies the aggregation idea correctly but uses the wrong probability basis.

distractors.text: Adding the value of each possible outcome and then multiplying the total by the highest probability
distractors.trap: Mistakenly treats EMV as a single weighted total rather than a sum of individually weighted outcomes.

explanation: EMV is a quantitative risk analysis technique that calculates an average expected result by weighting each possible outcome by its probability and summing the results. This is why both parts matter: the multiplication and the addition across outcomes. Distractors either use probability of non-occurrence or omit the summation step.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis
The Standard for Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects, quantitative analysis concepts

prompts: Finish-to-start means what in a project schedule network diagram?
In precedence diagramming, what does a finish-to-start relationship indicate between two activities?

correct.text: An activity cannot start until another has finished.
correct.why: A finish-to-start dependency means the predecessor must finish before the successor can begin.

distractors.text: An activity cannot finish until another has started.
distractors.trap: Confuses finish-to-start with start-to-finish logic.

distractors.text: An activity cannot start until another has started.
distractors.trap: Confuses finish-to-start with start-to-start logic.

distractors.text: An activity cannot finish until another has finished.
distractors.trap: Confuses finish-to-start with finish-to-finish logic.

distractors.text: Two activities must start and finish at the same time.
distractors.trap: Assumes dependencies always imply simultaneous execution rather than a defined sequencing rule.

explanation: Finish-to-start is the most common logical relationship in schedule network diagrams. It means the successor activity is blocked from starting until the predecessor activity is complete. The incorrect options each describe different dependency types or an invalid interpretation of schedule logic.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Sequence Activities
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Precedence Diagramming Method

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: According to the Scrum Guide, Product Backlog refinement is the act of adding detail, estimates, and order to items in the Product Backlog. Which statement best describes Product Backlog refinement?
In Scrum, what does Product Backlog refinement involve for Product Backlog items according to the Scrum Guide?

correct.text: Adding detail, estimates, and order
correct.why: This matches the Scrum Guide definition of Product Backlog refinement.

distractors.text: Adding detail and estimates only
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that ordering is separate from refinement rather than part of it.

distractors.text: Adding estimates and order only
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that refinement does not include clarifying item detail.

distractors.text: Adding detail and order only
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that estimation is optional within the definition itself.

distractors.text: Finalizing scope so backlog items no longer change
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that Scrum backlog work is fixed rather than continuously refined.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines Product Backlog refinement as adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items. The other choices each omit a required element or confuse refinement with freezing scope. The concept being tested is the exact definition of refinement in Scrum.
references: The Scrum Guide, Product Backlog, refinement
Agile Practice Guide, Agile Artifacts and Backlog Management

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project has a BAC of 15000, an EAC of 18000, and an SPI of 0.95. How should the project status be assessed?
Given BAC of 15000, EAC of 18000, and SPI of 0.95, what is the best assessment of the project's schedule and cost status?

correct.text: The project is behind schedule and over budget.
correct.why: An SPI below 1.0 indicates the project is behind schedule, and an EAC greater than BAC indicates it is forecast to finish over budget.

distractors.text: The project is ahead of schedule and over budget.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that any active progress implies schedule acceleration despite SPI being below 1.0.

distractors.text: The project is on schedule and on budget.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that BAC and EAC are interchangeable baseline and forecast values.

distractors.text: The project is ahead of schedule and under budget.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that the project is performing favorably even though both indicators show the opposite.

distractors.text: The project is behind schedule but under budget.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that budget status can be favorable when EAC exceeds BAC.

explanation: SPI of 0.95 means less work has been completed than planned, so the project is behind schedule. EAC of 18000 compared with BAC of 15000 means the forecasted final cost exceeds the approved budget. Together, these show the project is behind schedule and over budget.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Schedule Management, Control Schedule
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitor and Control Project Work

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
subtopic: CPI inference from BAC and EAC
prompts: A project has a BAC of 150000, an EAC of 180000, and an SPI of 1.0. What can be inferred about the CPI, and what action is most appropriate?
Given BAC of 150000, EAC of 180000, and SPI of 1.0, what does this imply about CPI and the needed management response?

correct.text: CPI is less than 1.0, indicating the need for budget management strategies.
correct.why: If EAC is greater than BAC, the project is forecast to exceed budget, which indicates cost inefficiency and therefore a CPI below 1.0.

distractors.text: CPI is equal to 1.0, suggesting the project is on budget.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that SPI of 1.0 also means cost performance is on target.

distractors.text: CPI is greater than 1.0, indicating efficient cost management.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that a favorable schedule condition implies favorable cost efficiency.

distractors.text: CPI cannot be determined without additional information.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that no reasonable cost inference can be made from a forecast that exceeds the budget.

distractors.text: CPI is less than 1.0, so the project should focus only on schedule recovery.
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that cost inefficiency should be answered with schedule action instead of budget control.

explanation: An SPI of 1.0 means the project is on schedule, but it says nothing positive about cost by itself. Because the EAC exceeds the BAC, the project is forecast to finish over budget, implying cost inefficiency and therefore a CPI below 1.0. The appropriate response is to apply budget management or cost control strategies.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Earned Value Analysis and Forecasting
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitor and Control Project Work

prompts: A project manager is strengthening the team's risk practices on a cross-functional project. How does a clear understanding of project terminology contribute to risk management in projects?
On a project with multiple stakeholders and technical disciplines, how does shared understanding of project terminology help the team manage risk?

correct.text: It allows project managers to identify and mitigate potential risks effectively.
correct.why: Consistent terminology improves communication and understanding, which helps teams recognize risks early and respond appropriately.

distractors.text: It complicates communication among team members.
distractors.trap: Assumes standard terminology creates confusion rather than reducing ambiguity.

distractors.text: It has no impact on risk management.
distractors.trap: Mistakenly treats language alignment as unrelated to risk identification and analysis.

distractors.text: It increases the likelihood of project failure.
distractors.trap: Confuses better communication practices with negative project outcomes.

distractors.text: It mainly reduces documentation effort without affecting risk responses.
distractors.trap: Misunderstands terminology as an administrative shortcut instead of a tool for shared risk understanding.

explanation: Risk management depends on accurate identification, analysis, and response planning, all of which are easier when stakeholders use the same terminology. A shared vocabulary reduces misunderstandings about threats, impacts, probability, and response actions. That common understanding supports more effective mitigation and monitoring throughout the project.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Uncertainty Performance Domain
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 11 Project Risk Management

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: During project performance review, the project manager sees that the Cost Performance Index or CPI is less than 1.0. How does a CPI value of less than 1.0 affect project management decisions?
A project dashboard shows CPI below 1.0 for the current reporting period. What does this mean for the project manager's next steps?

correct.text: It indicates that the project is over budget and corrective actions may be needed.
correct.why: A CPI below 1.0 means the project is earning less value than the money being spent, signaling cost inefficiency.

distractors.text: It implies that the project has no financial constraints.
distractors.trap: Confuses a cost performance metric with unrestricted funding.

distractors.text: It suggests that the project is under budget and ahead of schedule.
distractors.trap: Incorrectly reverses the meaning of CPI and mixes cost and schedule performance.

distractors.text: It shows that the project is on track and within budget.
distractors.trap: Assumes any stable metric indicates healthy performance without interpreting the threshold.

distractors.text: It means schedule variance is positive even if spending is high.
distractors.trap: Mistakes CPI for a schedule indicator rather than a cost efficiency measure.

explanation: CPI is a core earned value metric used to assess cost efficiency. When CPI is below 1.0, the project is spending more than the value of work accomplished, so the project manager should investigate causes and consider corrective action. This interpretation supports informed budget control decisions.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 7 Project Cost Management
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 7.4 Control Costs

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A project team is comparing delivery approaches for a product with evolving stakeholder needs. How does incremental delivery benefit project management compared to traditional delivery methods?
When stakeholder feedback is important throughout execution, how does incremental delivery benefit a project compared with a more traditional delivery approach?

correct.text: Incremental delivery provides usable results in stages with regular feedback, improving adaptability and stakeholder satisfaction.
correct.why: Delivering in increments enables earlier value delivery, faster feedback, and adjustment as needs evolve.

distractors.text: Incremental delivery reduces the need for planning, which tends to increase the chance of project failure.
distractors.trap: Assumes adaptive delivery eliminates planning rather than changing how planning is performed.

distractors.text: Incremental delivery removes the need for ongoing stakeholder involvement during execution.
distractors.trap: Mistakenly assumes iterative feedback cycles reduce stakeholder engagement.

distractors.text: Incremental delivery is used mainly as a way to reduce the overall project cost.
distractors.trap: Narrows the purpose of incremental delivery to one outcome and ignores value and adaptability.

distractors.text: Incremental delivery withholds any usable results until every increment has been finished.
distractors.trap: Confuses incremental delivery with a single end-of-project release.

explanation: Incremental delivery provides usable portions of the product over time rather than waiting until the end. That approach supports learning, adaptation, and ongoing stakeholder input, which is especially valuable when requirements may evolve. Compared with traditional delivery, it can improve responsiveness and stakeholder satisfaction.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Section 3 Life Cycle Selection
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain

prompts: How does Planned Value contribute to project performance measurement?
What is the primary role of Planned Value in measuring project performance?

correct.text: Planned Value helps assess whether the project is on schedule by comparing the planned work with actual progress.
correct.why: Planned Value provides the baseline value of scheduled work, which supports schedule performance assessment when compared with actual progress measures.

distractors.text: Planned Value indicates the total approved budget available for the entire project.
distractors.trap: Confuses Planned Value with the total project budget or Budget at Completion.

distractors.text: Planned Value is used to determine how effective the project manager is at leading the team.
distractors.trap: Mistakes an earned value performance metric for a personnel evaluation measure.

distractors.text: Planned Value measures the quality of the work completed by the project team.
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule and cost baseline metrics with quality measurement.

distractors.text: Planned Value shows the actual cost spent to complete the work performed to date.
distractors.trap: Confuses Planned Value with Actual Cost.

explanation: Planned Value is an earned value management concept representing the authorized budget assigned to scheduled work. It is especially useful in assessing schedule performance when considered alongside actual progress and earned value. It does not measure quality, leadership effectiveness, or the total budget by itself.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4 Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Earned Value Analysis

prompts: How often should risk identification take place on a project?
When should a project team identify risks during the life of a project?

correct.text: Throughout the project
correct.why: Risk identification is iterative because new risks can emerge and existing risks can change at any point in the project.

distractors.text: Only when the project manager has available time outside of core project work
distractors.trap: Treats risk identification as optional rather than a continuous project management activity.

distractors.text: One phase is enough if the project is large and already well defined
distractors.trap: Assumes size or early definition eliminates the need to revisit risks.

distractors.text: Only in the planning phase before execution begins
distractors.trap: Confuses initial risk planning with ongoing risk identification and monitoring.

distractors.text: Only after a major issue occurs and the team needs to respond
distractors.trap: Mistakes reactive issue management for proactive risk management.

explanation: PMI risk management practice treats risk identification as a recurring activity rather than a one-time event. Projects evolve, assumptions change, and external conditions shift, so risks must be identified and reviewed continuously. Limiting identification to planning or to ad hoc moments leaves the project exposed.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11 Project Risk Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11.2 Identify Risks

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A company approves work to create a new software application with a defined timeline and a specific set of features. In project management terms, how should this initiative be classified?
An organization starts developing a software product that has a clear start and finish date and defined deliverables. How is this effort classified in PMI terms?

correct.text: As a project
correct.why: The work is temporary and intended to create a unique product with defined deliverables, which matches PMI's definition of a project.

distractors.text: As an operation
distractors.trap: Confuses temporary, unique work with ongoing and repetitive business activities.

distractors.text: As a portfolio
distractors.trap: Mistakes a single initiative for a strategic grouping of projects, programs, and other work.

distractors.text: As a program
distractors.trap: Assumes one effort with defined features is automatically a coordinated set of related projects.

distractors.text: As a product line
distractors.trap: Mixes a business or marketing construct with the PMI classification of work.

explanation: PMI defines a project as a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result. A new software application with a defined timeline and specific features fits that definition. Operations are ongoing, while programs and portfolios involve coordination or governance across multiple initiatives rather than one discrete effort.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.1
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.3

prompts: During an iteration, a developer proposes a new requirement to the product owner, but the product owner says it is not a priority right now. What should the developer do next?
A team member identifies a useful new requirement, but the product owner decides it should not be included in the current iteration. What is the most appropriate next action?

correct.text: Document the requirement and monitor for future iterations.
correct.why: If the product owner does not prioritize the item for the current iteration, it should be captured and reconsidered through normal backlog management for future work.

distractors.text: Ignore the requirement since it is not a priority.
distractors.trap: Assumes lower current priority means the idea has no future value and should be discarded.

distractors.text: Present the requirement again in the next team meeting.
distractors.trap: Confuses collaborative discussion with bypassing the product owner's prioritization decision.

distractors.text: Implement the requirement anyway to demonstrate its value.
distractors.trap: Represents unauthorized scope change and ignores product owner accountability for prioritization.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue to the sponsor so the requirement can be added immediately.
distractors.trap: Treats normal backlog prioritization as a governance failure requiring escalation.

explanation: In agile work, new ideas are expected, but they still need to be prioritized through the product backlog. When the product owner decides an item is not a current priority, the appropriate response is to capture it and revisit it in future backlog refinement or planning. Team members should not ignore potentially valuable work, but they also should not implement or force it outside the agreed prioritization process.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Section 3.1
Agile Practice Guide, Section 5.2

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
subtopic: CPI and cost control under schedule compression
prompts: A project has a CPI of 0.7, and the project manager decides to authorize overtime to help meet deadlines. What should the project manager monitor most closely to help ensure project success?
A project is running with a CPI of 0.7, and overtime is introduced to recover schedule performance. Which factor should the project manager watch most closely?

correct.text: Overall project costs
correct.why: With a CPI below 1.0, the project is already cost inefficient, and adding overtime can further increase actual costs.

distractors.text: Project scope changes
distractors.trap: This assumes the primary risk from overtime is scope instability rather than worsening cost performance.

distractors.text: Quality of deliverables
distractors.trap: This reflects the misconception that quality is the most immediate control point when the scenario highlights poor cost efficiency.

distractors.text: Team member satisfaction
distractors.trap: This confuses an important people concern with the key performance area most directly threatened by overtime and a low CPI.

distractors.text: Stakeholder communication frequency
distractors.trap: This mistakes a general management action for the metric most directly impacted by overtime in an over-budget project.

explanation: A CPI of 0.7 means the project is getting only 70 cents of value for every dollar spent, so it is already over budget. Overtime is a schedule compression technique that often raises labor costs, making cost control the most critical area to monitor. Quality and team morale still matter, but they are not the best answer to the scenario presented.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4 Control Costs
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4.2.2 Earned Value Analysis
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.6 Control Schedule

prompts: A project experiences an unexpected delay caused by a natural disaster. How should management reserve be used in this situation?
During a project, a natural disaster causes an unforeseen delay. What is the appropriate use of management reserve?

correct.text: It should be used to cover additional costs incurred due to the delay.
correct.why: Management reserve is intended for unforeseen work or events such as unknown risks, including impacts from a natural disaster.

distractors.text: It should be returned to the project budget as savings.
distractors.trap: This assumes reserve should be released rather than used when an unplanned event actually occurs.

distractors.text: It should be set aside for future projects.
distractors.trap: This treats reserve as a portfolio savings mechanism instead of funding for current project uncertainty.

distractors.text: It should be used only after contingency reserve is fully exhausted regardless of cause.
distractors.trap: This incorrectly treats all reserve decisions as purely sequential and ignores that management reserve addresses unknown unknowns.

distractors.text: It should be applied only to recover lost schedule time, not added cost.
distractors.trap: This confuses schedule recovery actions with the budget purpose of reserve funds.

explanation: Management reserve is budget held for unforeseen work that is within the scope of the project but not included in the cost baseline. A natural disaster delay is an example of an unexpected event that can create additional costs, so management reserve may be used to address that impact. The other choices misunderstand the purpose of reserve funding.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.3 Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.3.2.6 Reserve Analysis
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 11 Project Risk Management

prompts: A project has an earned value of 50000 and a planned value of 60000. What is the Schedule Performance Index or SPI?
For a project with EV of 50000 and PV of 60000, what is the SPI?

correct.why: SPI is calculated as EV divided by PV, so 50000 divided by 60000 equals 0.83 when rounded.

distractors.trap: This reflects an incorrect calculation or the use of the wrong values in the SPI formula.
This inverts the SPI formula by dividing PV by EV instead of EV by PV.
This represents a formula error and incorrectly suggests the project is significantly ahead of schedule.
This assumes the project is on schedule without performing the earned value calculation.

explanation: SPI is the ratio of earned value to planned value. In this case, 50000 divided by 60000 equals 0.83, which indicates the project is progressing at 83 percent of the planned rate. Because SPI is less than 1.0, the project is behind schedule.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.6 Control Schedule
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4.2.2 Earned Value Analysis

prompts: A project is behind schedule, and the team is considering crashing selected activities to recover time. What should be the primary consideration when deciding which activities to crash?
A project manager needs to shorten a delayed project by crashing some work activities. Which factor should guide the selection of activities to crash first?

correct.text: The impact on the project's critical path
correct.why: Crashing only improves the overall project finish date when it shortens activities on the critical path.

distractors.text: The preferences of the stakeholders
distractors.trap: Assumes schedule compression decisions should be driven mainly by stakeholder opinion rather than schedule logic.

distractors.text: The availability of team members
distractors.trap: Confuses a resource constraint with the main criterion for whether crashing will reduce total project duration.

distractors.text: The budget allocated for the project
distractors.trap: Assumes cost is the first selection criterion instead of whether the activity affects the project completion date.

distractors.text: The activities with the lowest risk
distractors.trap: Mistakes risk preference for the core principle that only critical path work shortens the schedule end date.

explanation: When a team crashes activities, the first concern is whether shortening those activities will actually reduce the overall project duration. That occurs only when the activities are on the critical path. Budget, resources, and risk still matter, but they are secondary to the basic schedule logic.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Schedule Management, Develop Schedule, Schedule Compression
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models, Methods, and Artifacts, Critical Path Method
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Develop Schedule

prompts: A project is behind schedule. How should the project manager use Planned Value to address the situation?
A delayed project requires performance analysis. How should the project manager use Planned Value along with other earned value data to determine what action to take?

correct.text: The Project Manager should analyze the Planned Value against the Actual Cost and Earned Value to identify discrepancies and adjust the project plan.
correct.why: Planned Value is used with Earned Value and Actual Cost to evaluate schedule and cost performance and support corrective action.

distractors.text: The Project Manager should increase the project budget to absorb the delays and keep spending on track.
distractors.trap: Assumes more funding alone resolves schedule performance without first analyzing the underlying variance.

distractors.text: The Project Manager should set Planned Value aside and concentrate only on upcoming tasks and deliverables.
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that forecast-driven action can replace performance measurement and variance analysis.

distractors.text: The Project Manager should simply report the schedule delays to stakeholders without performing any variance analysis.
distractors.trap: Confuses communication with management action and skips the needed performance analysis.

distractors.text: The Project Manager should compare Planned Value only against the approved total budget rather than actual results.
distractors.trap: Misunderstands earned value management by treating PV as a standalone budget figure instead of part of an integrated analysis.

explanation: Planned Value becomes useful when compared with Earned Value and Actual Cost to reveal whether the project is underperforming in schedule, cost, or both. That analysis helps the project manager identify discrepancies and decide on corrective actions. Merely increasing budget or reporting status does not address the root issue.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Control Costs, Earned Value Analysis
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain, Earned Value Analysis
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group, Control Costs

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is experiencing cost overruns. How should the cost baseline be used to address the issue?
When actual spending exceeds expectations on a project, what is the appropriate use of the cost baseline?

correct.text: The cost baseline would be used to compare actual costs against planned costs to identify variances and take corrective actions.
correct.why: The cost baseline is the approved standard for measuring project cost performance and determining needed corrective action.

distractors.text: The cost baseline would be used to justify expanding the project scope in order to accommodate the overruns.
distractors.trap: Confuses budget control with scope expansion and assumes overruns justify adding more work.

distractors.text: The cost baseline would be set aside and ignored because it is now considered outdated.
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that a baseline loses value once variances appear.

distractors.text: The cost baseline would be immediately adjusted to match the new estimated costs without any variance analysis.
distractors.trap: Assumes baselines should be changed immediately to match actuals rather than used first for variance analysis and control.

distractors.text: The cost baseline would be used only for final reporting once the project has been completed.
distractors.trap: Mistakes the baseline as a historical record only, instead of an active control tool during execution.

explanation: The cost baseline is the approved version of the time-phased project budget used to measure actual performance. When overruns occur, the project manager compares actual costs with the baseline to understand variances and determine corrective action. The baseline should not simply be ignored or reset without analysis and change control.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Determine Budget
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Cost Management, Control Costs
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group, Control Costs

prompts: A project is falling behind schedule. What should the project manager do to address the delay?
A project manager sees that the project is slipping against the schedule baseline. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Analyze the schedule variance and apply recovery techniques such as fast-tracking or crashing as appropriate.
correct.why: The project manager should first analyze schedule performance, then compress the schedule through controlled corrective actions such as fast-tracking, crashing, or reallocating resources.

distractors.text: Reduce the project scope by removing tasks to make up the lost time.
distractors.trap: Assumes scope reduction is the default response to schedule slippage rather than a controlled change decision.

distractors.text: Ignore the delays and keep following the original schedule baseline.
distractors.trap: Represents passive management and failure to monitor and control schedule performance.

distractors.text: Increase the project budget without adjusting the schedule or the work plan.
distractors.trap: Assumes spending more money alone will fix delay without revising the actual work plan.

distractors.text: Postpone status reporting until the schedule stabilizes on its own.
distractors.trap: Mistakes withholding information for problem solving and undermines timely monitoring and control.

explanation: The correct response to schedule slippage is to analyze the variance and apply recovery techniques such as fast-tracking, crashing, resequencing, or reallocating resources through controlled corrective action. Ignoring the delay or changing unrelated constraints without analysis is not effective project control.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Schedule Management, Control Schedule
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group, Control Schedule

prompts: A project is falling behind schedule. Which process group is most relevant for addressing this issue, and what should the project manager do next?
A project manager sees that the project is slipping against the schedule baseline. Which process group should be used to address the problem, and what action is most appropriate?

correct.text: The Monitoring process group; the project manager should analyze performance data and implement corrective actions.
correct.why: Schedule variance is addressed by monitoring performance against the baseline and taking corrective action when needed.

distractors.text: The Executing process group; the project manager should increase team resources.
distractors.trap: Assumes adding resources is always the first response instead of first analyzing performance and variance.

distractors.text: The Closing process group; the project manager should finalize the project documentation.
distractors.trap: Confuses an active schedule problem with end-of-project administrative closure.

distractors.text: The Planning process group; the project manager should revise the project schedule.
distractors.trap: Assumes the schedule should be revised immediately rather than first controlled through monitoring and corrective action.

distractors.text: The Initiating process group; the project manager should reconfirm the business case.
distractors.trap: Mistakes a delivery performance issue for an initiation activity.

explanation: When a project is behind schedule, the key work is to compare actual performance with the schedule baseline, determine the cause of the variance, and take corrective action. That aligns with the Monitoring process group and schedule control activities. Simply adding resources or revising plans without analysis can create new issues and does not reflect disciplined project control.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.6 Control Schedule
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Measurement Performance Domain

prompts: A project manager decides to include a requested pilot technology in the project scope. What should the project manager do next to manage the project effectively?
A stakeholder requests that a pilot technology be added, and the project manager agrees to include it in scope. What is the next best step?

correct.text: Assess the impact of the change on the project schedule and budget.
correct.why: Approved or proposed scope changes should be evaluated for their effects on key constraints before implementation.

distractors.text: Communicate the change to the project team only.
distractors.trap: Assumes notification alone is enough and ignores impact analysis and broader stakeholder implications.

distractors.text: Ignore the request if it exceeds the original scope.
distractors.trap: Treats all scope additions as automatic rejection rather than managed change.

distractors.text: Immediately implement the change without further analysis.
distractors.trap: Assumes accepted changes can be executed without evaluating effects on constraints and plans.

distractors.text: Update the lessons learned repository before doing anything else.
distractors.trap: Confuses change assessment with organizational knowledge capture.

explanation: Once a scope change is being considered or accepted, the project manager should evaluate its impact on schedule, budget, and other constraints so informed decisions can be made. This reflects integrated change control and disciplined scope management. Communicating or implementing first skips analysis and increases project risk.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.6 Control Scope
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group

prompts: A project manager discovers a recurring issue during project execution. What should the project manager do to address it effectively?
During execution, the same problem keeps happening on the project. What is the most effective action for the project manager to take?

correct.text: Capture the findings in the lessons learned repository.
correct.why: Recurring issues should be documented so the team and organization can understand causes and avoid repeating them.

distractors.text: Reassign the team members involved.
distractors.trap: Assumes recurring issues are primarily personnel problems rather than process or knowledge issues.

distractors.text: Ignore the issue to avoid conflict.
distractors.trap: Avoids issue management and confuses harmony with effective leadership.

distractors.text: Increase the project budget to accommodate errors.
distractors.trap: Treats the symptom with more funding instead of learning from and addressing the cause.

distractors.text: Close the issue log entry without further action.
distractors.trap: Assumes recurring problems can be administratively closed without organizational learning.

explanation: A recurring issue signals a pattern that should be documented and analyzed so the project team can improve future performance. Capturing it in the lessons learned repository supports continuous improvement and knowledge transfer. Reassignment, avoidance, or added budget do not address the underlying learning need.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.4 Manage Project Knowledge
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Lessons Learned Register
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Executing Process Group

prompts: A project manager discovers that a team member implemented unauthorized changes to the project scope. What should the project manager do to address the issue effectively?
During project execution, a team member makes scope changes without approval. What is the most appropriate response from the project manager?

correct.text: Remove the unauthorized changes and reinforce adherence to the project scope with the team.
correct.why: Unapproved scope changes should be reversed or corrected and the team reminded to follow scope and change control procedures.

distractors.text: Discuss the changes with the client and seek approval.
distractors.trap: Assumes a change can be legitimized after unauthorized implementation instead of first restoring control.

distractors.text: Allow the changes to remain and assess their impact later.
distractors.trap: Treats scope discipline as optional and delays corrective action against scope creep.

distractors.text: Ignore the changes if they do not appear to affect the overall project outcome.
distractors.trap: Assumes informal judgment can replace approved scope management processes.

distractors.text: Add the changes to the lessons learned register and continue execution.
distractors.trap: Confuses documenting an issue with actually controlling scope and correcting deviation.

explanation: Unauthorized scope changes undermine scope baselines and formal change control. The project manager should restore alignment with the approved scope and reinforce expected team behavior so future changes follow the proper process. Simply accepting the change later or ignoring it invites scope creep.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Control Scope
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Perform Integrated Change Control
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Control Scope

prompts: A client requests a significant change, but the project manager does not follow the integrated change control process. What issue is most likely to result?
If a project manager bypasses integrated change control after a major client requested change, what is the most likely consequence?

correct.text: Uncontrolled changes leading to scope creep and budget overruns
correct.why: Integrated change control exists to evaluate and govern changes so they do not expand scope and cost without authorization.

distractors.text: Immediate project completion ahead of schedule
distractors.trap: Assumes skipping governance accelerates delivery without negative consequences.

distractors.text: Enhanced client satisfaction without further steps
distractors.trap: Assumes accepting change informally always improves outcomes and removes the need for evaluation.

distractors.text: Increased team morale and productivity
distractors.trap: Assumes unmanaged changes help team performance rather than create confusion and rework.

distractors.text: Automatic improvement in quality because the client initiated the change
distractors.trap: Assumes client requested changes are inherently beneficial and need no integrated assessment.

explanation: Integrated change control protects the project from unmanaged impacts to scope, schedule, cost, and other baselines. When a significant change is implemented without that process, the project is exposed to uncontrolled expansion of work and cost overruns. Bypassing control does not guarantee faster delivery, happier stakeholders, or better quality.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Perform Integrated Change Control
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Monitor and Control Project Work
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Integrated Change Control

prompts: A project manager begins executing a project without a project charter. What problem is most likely to arise?
If a project does not have a project charter, what is the most likely issue during execution for the project manager?

correct.text: Lack of authority to allocate resources
correct.why: The project charter formally authorizes the project and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources.

distractors.text: Inaccurate risk assessment
distractors.trap: Confuses the purpose of the charter with the detailed analysis performed in risk management planning.

distractors.text: Insufficient stakeholder engagement
distractors.trap: Assumes the charter directly replaces stakeholder engagement planning and management.

distractors.text: Inability to meet deadlines
distractors.trap: Assumes schedule failure is the primary direct result of a missing charter rather than missing formal authority.

distractors.text: Inability to create status reports
distractors.trap: Confuses project authorization with routine project communications.

explanation: The project charter is the document that formally authorizes the project and the project manager's use of resources. Without it, the project manager may lack clear authority to secure or allocate those resources during execution. Other issues may occur, but the most direct consequence is lack of formal authority.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Develop Project Charter
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Develop Project Charter
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Project Work Performance Domain

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager faces a sudden shortage of team members because several people are out sick. Which method is most effective to help ensure project continuity, and why?
During project execution, unexpected illness reduces the available workforce. What is the best method for maintaining continuity, and why is it appropriate?

correct.text: Resource leveling, because it allows for the adjustment of task schedules to accommodate the reduced workforce.
correct.why: Resource leveling helps rebalance the schedule when resource availability is constrained, making it appropriate when team capacity suddenly drops.

distractors.text: Agile ceremonies, because holding more of them improves communication and coordination across the team.
distractors.trap: Assumes better communication alone resolves a concrete resource capacity shortage.

distractors.text: Earned value management, because it tracks cost and schedule performance against the baseline.
distractors.trap: Confuses performance measurement with a technique for adjusting work to limited resource availability.

distractors.text: Scope creep, because adding new tasks to the plan would help fill the workforce gap.
distractors.trap: Misunderstands scope creep as a helpful planning method rather than an uncontrolled expansion of work.

distractors.text: Quality audits, because reviewing deliverables against standards would offset the missing staff.
distractors.trap: Assumes quality assurance activities solve staffing shortages and schedule pressure.

explanation: When available staff suddenly decreases, the project manager needs a method that directly addresses limited resource capacity. Resource leveling adjusts activity timing based on actual resource availability, helping the team continue with a realistic schedule. The other options may support management in general, but they do not directly resolve the shortage.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Develop Schedule
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Sixth Edition, Estimate Activity Resources
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Develop Schedule

prompts: If a project manager fails to implement effective scope management, what is a likely consequence for the project?
A project manager does not manage scope effectively during the project. What is the most likely consequence?

correct.text: Increased risk of scope creep
correct.why: Without effective scope management, uncontrolled additions and changes to project work become more likely.

distractors.text: Faster project completion
distractors.trap: Assumes weak scope control accelerates delivery rather than creating extra work and rework.

distractors.text: Improved stakeholder satisfaction
distractors.trap: Mistakes uncontrolled changes for responsiveness, ignoring the confusion and instability they create.

distractors.text: Reduced project costs
distractors.trap: Assumes less discipline in scope management lowers cost instead of increasing it through unplanned work.

distractors.text: Automatic improvement in quality performance
distractors.trap: Confuses flexibility in scope with stronger quality control.

explanation: Scope management establishes how requirements and changes are defined, controlled, and validated. If that discipline is missing, the project is more vulnerable to unauthorized or poorly evaluated additions to work, which is the essence of scope creep. Scope creep often drives schedule, cost, and quality problems afterward.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Scope Management, Plan Scope Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Scope Management, Control Scope
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group

subtopic: Root cause analysis for stakeholder engagement decline
prompts: If a project manager finds that stakeholder engagement is declining despite using regular updates, which combination of tools should they implement to investigate the issue further?
A project manager is sending regular stakeholder updates, but engagement continues to decline. Which tools are the best combination to investigate the underlying cause?

correct.text: Ishikawa diagram
correct.why: An Ishikawa diagram helps organize and analyze possible causes behind declining stakeholder engagement.

correct.text: Five whys
correct.why: Five whys helps drill down from visible symptoms to the underlying root cause of the engagement problem.

distractors.text: Open space meeting
distractors.trap: Assumes a facilitation format is itself a root cause analysis technique.

distractors.text: SWOT analysis
distractors.trap: Uses a strategic assessment tool instead of a method for diagnosing the cause of a specific issue.

distractors.text: Gantt chart
distractors.trap: Confuses schedule planning tools with problem investigation techniques.

distractors.text: Risk register
distractors.trap: Treats a repository of identified risks as a tool for uncovering the root cause of a current issue.

distractors.text: Communication management plan
distractors.trap: Assumes an existing planning artifact can by itself diagnose why engagement is dropping.

explanation: When engagement declines despite routine updates, the problem is not just frequency of communication but the underlying cause of reduced stakeholder response. Ishikawa diagram and Five whys are complementary root cause analysis tools that help identify why current communication is not working. The other options may support project management in other ways, but they do not directly investigate root causes.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Quality Management, Control Quality
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Cause-and-Effect Diagram
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models Methods and Artifacts, Root Cause Analysis

prompts: A project manager identifies a delay in an activity on the critical path. What immediate action should the project manager take?
A schedule issue occurs on a critical path activity. What should the project manager do first to understand the effect on the project end date?

correct.text: Analyze the critical path to determine how much the delay affects the project completion date
correct.why: A delay on the critical path can directly push out the finish date, so the first step is to analyze the critical path and quantify the schedule impact.

distractors.text: Immediately change the project scope to work around the delay
distractors.trap: Assumes scope changes are an immediate schedule control response without first analyzing impact or following formal change processes.

distractors.text: Ignore the delay as long as other activities remain on schedule
distractors.trap: Confuses overall activity progress with critical path logic and overlooks that critical path delays can delay the whole project.

distractors.text: Increase the project budget to accommodate the delay
distractors.trap: Assumes spending more money is the first response before understanding whether and how the delay affects completion.

distractors.text: Have the team focus on noncritical activities first until the delay resolves
distractors.trap: Mistakes busy work for schedule recovery and ignores the need to evaluate the impact on the critical path.

explanation: When an activity on the critical path is delayed, the project manager should first evaluate how that delay affects the project completion date. Critical path activities have no float or limited float, so a delay may immediately threaten the schedule baseline. Corrective action should come after understanding the impact.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Develop Schedule, Critical Path Method
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.6 Control Schedule
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning Process Group, Develop Schedule

prompts: A project manager is tasked with improving communication within a project team. Which knowledge area should the project manager focus on?
A team is experiencing communication problems on a project. Which project management knowledge area is most directly concerned with improving this?

correct.text: Information distribution and stakeholder engagement management
correct.why: Improving how project information is planned, shared, received, and managed falls under project communications management.

distractors.text: Quality management
distractors.trap: Confuses the quality of deliverables and processes with the management of information exchange among stakeholders.

distractors.text: Scope management
distractors.trap: Assumes the issue is primarily about defining project boundaries and requirements.

distractors.text: Risk management
distractors.trap: Treats the problem as mainly uncertainty events rather than a core information-exchange concern.

distractors.text: Procurement management
distractors.trap: Confuses vendor acquisition and contract management with internal team information sharing.

explanation: The knowledge area that directly addresses how information is generated, distributed, stored, and monitored is project communications management. While it can affect quality, scope, and risk outcomes, those areas are not the primary focus of this question. The best answer is the knowledge area specifically centered on managing information exchange among stakeholders.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Communications Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 10.1 Plan Communications Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 10.2 Manage Communications

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/Methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is transitioning from a traditional project management approach to Agile. What tool might replace Gantt charts for tracking progress?
A team moves from a predictive approach to Agile and needs a different way to visualize progress instead of a Gantt chart. Which tool is most appropriate?

correct.text: Burndown Chart
correct.why: A burndown chart is a common Agile tool used to track remaining work and progress over time.

distractors.text: Gantt Chart
distractors.trap: Assumes the same predictive scheduling artifact remains the primary Agile progress tracking tool.

distractors.text: Kanban Board
distractors.trap: Confuses workflow visualization with the specific Agile chart used to track remaining work over time.

distractors.text: Critical Path Method
distractors.trap: Represents the misconception that predictive schedule network analysis is the Agile replacement for progress tracking.

distractors.text: Resource Histogram
distractors.trap: Confuses resource planning artifacts with Agile progress measurement tools.

explanation: In Agile environments, teams commonly use burndown charts to show how much work remains across an iteration or release. A Kanban board can visualize workflow, but it does not serve the same purpose as a burndown chart in showing remaining effort over time. Predictive tools such as Gantt charts and critical path analysis are not the closest Agile replacement here.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Section 3, Agile, Lean, and Hybrid Approaches
Agile Practice Guide, Section 5, Delivering in an Agile Environment
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Models, Methods, and Artifacts, Burndown Chart

prompts: A project is using a predictive methodology, and a significant change in requirements is identified in the middle of the project. What is the most appropriate action?
During a predictive project, major new requirements are discovered after execution is underway. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Assess the impact and follow change control processes to manage the change.
correct.why: In a predictive environment, significant requirement changes should be formally evaluated and processed through integrated change control.

distractors.text: Consult the team for consensus on the change.
distractors.trap: Assumes team agreement alone is sufficient without formal impact assessment and approved change control.

distractors.text: Immediately implement the change without further assessment.
distractors.trap: Confuses responsiveness with bypassing governance, analysis, and authorization.

distractors.text: Ignore the change as requirements are fixed.
distractors.trap: Mistakes predictive planning for absolute rigidity and ignores the need to evaluate valid change requests.

distractors.text: Delay discussing the change until project closing to avoid disruption.
distractors.trap: Assumes unresolved requirement changes can be postponed safely instead of being managed through the current control process.

explanation: In predictive projects, significant requirement changes are managed through formal change control rather than implemented informally. The project manager should assess the effect on scope, schedule, cost, risk, and other baselines, then follow the approved governance process. Neither immediate implementation nor ignoring the change is appropriate.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.6 Control Scope
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group, Perform Integrated Change Control

prompts: A project manager needs to change the approved project scope. Which document should the project manager consult for guidance on what has been formally defined and approved?
During project execution, a request is raised to modify project scope. Which project document provides the approved scope reference that should guide evaluation of the change?

correct.text: Scope Baseline
correct.why: The scope baseline is the approved version of project scope against which proposed scope changes are evaluated and controlled.

distractors.text: Project charter
distractors.trap: Confuses the high-level authorization document with the detailed approved scope reference used for scope control.

distractors.text: Risk management plan
distractors.trap: Assumes any change-related issue should be handled through risk documents rather than scope control artifacts.

distractors.text: Stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Mistakes a stakeholder information artifact for a document that defines and governs approved scope.

distractors.text: Communications management plan
distractors.trap: Confuses how information is shared with what baseline is used to assess scope changes.

explanation: When scope changes are proposed, the project manager needs the approved scope reference to determine what is changing and whether formal control is required. The scope baseline serves that purpose because it captures the approved project scope and is used in monitoring and controlling scope. The other options support governance or planning in different areas but do not define the approved scope basis for change evaluation.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Scope Management, section 5.6 Control Scope
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 4.2 Develop Project Management Plan
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Monitoring and Controlling Process Group, scope baseline and change control

prompts: A project manager does not plan for ongoing risk identification throughout the project. What is the most likely impact on the project?
If continuous risk identification is neglected during project work, what consequence is most likely for project performance and outcomes?

correct.text: Increased likelihood of unforeseen risks affecting project outcomes
correct.why: Without ongoing risk identification, new and emerging risks are more likely to go unnoticed until they negatively affect objectives.

distractors.text: Enhanced team dynamics and collaboration
distractors.trap: Assumes less formal risk work automatically improves teamwork instead of increasing exposure to uncertainty.

distractors.text: Reduced need for risk management processes altogether
distractors.trap: Treats the absence of planning as evidence that risk management is unnecessary.

distractors.text: Improved stakeholder satisfaction due to fewer risks being identified
distractors.trap: Equates not identifying risks with actually reducing risk exposure or stakeholder concern.

distractors.text: Faster schedule performance because the team spends less time on analysis
distractors.trap: Assumes short-term efficiency gains outweigh the project impact of unmanaged uncertainty.

explanation: Risk identification is not a one-time event because project conditions change and new threats and opportunities emerge over time. If the team fails to continue identifying risks, important uncertainties may remain hidden until they disrupt cost, schedule, scope, or quality. Fewer identified risks does not mean the project is safer; it usually means visibility is worse.
references: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Project Risk Management, section 11.2 Identify Risks
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Performance Domains, Uncertainty
Process Groups A Practice Guide, Planning and Monitoring and Controlling Process Groups, risk identification and response

eco_task: Address and Remove Impediments, Obstacles, and Blockers for the Team
topic: Address and Remove Impediments, Obstacles, and Blockers for the Team
prompts: A project manager sees a downward trend in items completed on a Throughput Chart. What action should the project manager consider first?
A Throughput Chart shows that the team is completing fewer work items over time. What is the most appropriate immediate response from the project manager?

correct.text: Investigate potential bottlenecks or resource issues affecting productivity.
correct.why: A decline in throughput signals reduced flow, so the first step is to identify constraints or impediments limiting the team's delivery rate.

distractors.text: Reduce the scope of the project to simplify tasks.
distractors.trap: Jumps to changing scope before diagnosing the actual cause of the delivery slowdown.

distractors.text: Increase the number of tasks assigned to team members.
distractors.trap: Assumes pushing more work into the system will improve output rather than worsen overload and work in progress.

distractors.text: Extend the project timeline to accommodate delays.
distractors.trap: Treats the symptom by adjusting dates instead of addressing the underlying flow problem.

distractors.text: Escalate the declining trend to sponsors and wait for their guidance before taking any action.
distractors.trap: Defers to escalation before the team investigates and understands the root cause of the slowdown.

explanation: Throughput measures how many work items are completed in a given period, so a sustained decline suggests a system constraint, blocker, or capacity issue. The most effective first action is to investigate what is interrupting flow, such as bottlenecks, dependencies, resource gaps, or process friction. Changing scope, loading more work, extending time, or escalating before diagnosis may be premature and can worsen the problem if the root cause is not understood.
references: Agile Practice Guide, Measure Team Performance, flow based metrics including throughput
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Performance Domains, Delivery and Measurement
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Performance Domains, Team and impediment removal

subtopic: Earned value interpretation for schedule recovery
prompts: A project manager observes that the Schedule Performance Index remains below 1 while the Cost Performance Index stays above 1. What should be the primary focus of corrective action?
Project performance data shows SPI below 1 consistently and CPI above 1. Which area should the project manager prioritize for corrective action?

correct.text: Improving the project schedule
