correct.text: Defects (work that must be reworked or scrapped)
correct.why: Defects are a classic Lean waste, consuming effort on correction rather than value.

distractors.text: Standardized work
distractors.trap: Standardized work is a Lean practice for stabilizing a process, not a form of waste.

distractors.text: Customer value
distractors.trap: Delivering customer value is the goal of Lean, the opposite of waste.

distractors.text: Kaizen
distractors.trap: Kaizen is the Lean philosophy of continuous improvement, a method rather than a waste.

explanation: The wastes of muda include overproduction, waiting, transportation, over-processing, inventory, motion, and defects. Standardized work, customer value, and kaizen are Lean concepts and goals, not wastes.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Define completion and acceptance on an agile team
prompts: An agile team disputes two concepts. One is the shared, story-independent standard that all work must meet to be considered complete, owned by the development team. The other is the specific set of conditions a particular feature must satisfy to be accepted, owned by the product owner. Which mapping is correct?

correct.text: Definition of Done is the developers' universal quality bar applied to every backlog item; Acceptance Criteria are the product owner's pass/fail checks unique to each feature
correct.why: The DoD is a global quality bar owned by the developers; acceptance criteria are feature-specific and owned by the product owner.

distractors.text: Acceptance Criteria are the developers' global completion standard applied to all work; the Definition of Done is the product owner's set of per-story conditions
distractors.trap: Ownership and scope are reversed for both terms.

distractors.text: Both the Definition of Done and the Acceptance Criteria are owned and maintained by the product owner, who applies each of them at the individual story level
distractors.trap: The Definition of Done is owned by the development team, not the product owner.

distractors.text: Both are owned by the development team, with the Definition of Done set per feature and Acceptance Criteria serving as the release-wide global standard
distractors.trap: The product owner, not the development team, owns acceptance criteria, and the scopes of the two terms are swapped here.

explanation: Definition of Done is a team-owned, story-independent standard ensuring consistent quality across all work. Acceptance Criteria are product-owner-owned and unique to each story, defining what that feature must do to be accepted.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

prompts: A requirement states that the system must process a payment within two seconds and remain available 99.9 percent of the time. How is this requirement best classified?

correct.text: Non-functional requirement
correct.why: Performance and availability are quality attributes describing how well the system must behave, which makes them non-functional.

distractors.text: Functional requirement, because processing a payment is a function the system performs
distractors.trap: Payment processing is the function, but the two-second and availability targets are quality attributes, which are non-functional.

distractors.text: Business requirement, because it describes a high-level organizational need
distractors.trap: Business requirements state the why and the goals; this is a measurable solution quality attribute, not a business goal.

distractors.text: Stakeholder requirement, because availability matters to users
distractors.trap: Stakeholder requirements describe a stakeholder group's needs; specific performance and availability metrics are non-functional requirements.

explanation: Functional requirements describe what the system does; non-functional requirements describe quality attributes such as performance, availability, security, and usability. Response-time and uptime targets are classic non-functional requirements.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

prompts: A quality manager is categorizing cost-of-quality items for a project. Which item is a cost of conformance rather than a cost of non-conformance?

correct.text: Quality training and product inspection and testing
correct.why: Prevention costs (training) and appraisal costs (inspection and testing) are spent to avoid defects, making them costs of conformance.

distractors.text: Rework and scrap caused by internal defects
distractors.trap: These are internal failure costs, which are costs of non-conformance.

distractors.text: Warranty claims and product recalls
distractors.trap: These are external failure costs found after delivery, which are costs of non-conformance.

distractors.text: Liability and lost business from defects reaching the customer
distractors.trap: These are external failure costs, a category of cost of non-conformance.

explanation: Cost of conformance covers prevention (training, quality planning) and appraisal (inspection, testing) costs spent to prevent and detect defects. Cost of non-conformance covers internal failures (rework, scrap) and external failures (warranty, recalls, liability) when defects occur.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

prompts: A deliverable has passed the team's internal quality checks confirming it was built correctly. What is the next process, what is its purpose, and who performs it?

correct.text: Validate Scope, in which the customer or sponsor formally inspects and accepts the deliverable
correct.why: Validate Scope follows Control Quality and focuses on the customer's formal acceptance of completed deliverables.

distractors.text: Control Quality again, performed by QA, to re-verify correctness before release
distractors.trap: Control Quality precedes Validate Scope and checks correctness; the deliverable already passed it.

distractors.text: Close Project or Phase, since a correct deliverable can be closed without customer sign-off
distractors.trap: Formal acceptance through Validate Scope must occur before closure.

distractors.text: Direct and Manage Project Work, to begin producing the next deliverable
distractors.trap: This skips obtaining formal acceptance of the completed deliverable.

explanation: Control Quality verifies correctness against requirements; its verified deliverables then go to Validate Scope, where the customer or sponsor formally accepts them. Acceptance must precede closure.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

prompts: While decomposing scope into a WBS, a team lead proposes adding a work package for a feature that is not in the project scope statement, and quietly omitting a small piece of approved scope to save time. Which WBS principle is being violated?

correct.text: The 100 percent rule
correct.why: The WBS must capture 100 percent of the approved work and nothing outside it, so adding out-of-scope work or dropping approved scope violates it.

distractors.text: The rolling wave principle, which allows any work to be added later
distractors.trap: Rolling wave is progressive elaboration of detail over time, not a license to add out-of-scope work or drop approved scope.

distractors.text: The 8/80 rule, which dictates which features belong in scope
distractors.trap: The 8/80 heuristic guides work package size and duration, not whether something is in scope.

distractors.text: MoSCoW prioritization, which permits silently dropping low-priority approved scope
distractors.trap: Prioritization does not authorize unilaterally removing baselined scope without change control.

explanation: The 100 percent rule states that the WBS includes all of the work defined by the project scope, and only that work. Adding unauthorized features or omitting approved scope both break it.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Manage project knowledge across the lifecycle
prompts: During execution a team continually records insights as they occur. At closeout these are finalized and moved into an organizational asset so future projects can benefit. Which two artifacts are described, in order?

correct.text: The lessons learned register first, then the lessons learned repository as the organizational process asset that outlives the project
correct.why: The register is the live project document; at closeout its contents feed the lessons learned repository, an organizational process asset for future projects.

distractors.text: The lessons learned repository as the live project document, then the lessons learned register stored as the organizational asset
distractors.trap: Reversed: the register is the active project document and the repository is the organizational store.

distractors.text: The risk register during execution, then the issue log archived at closeout as the reusable organizational asset
distractors.trap: These track risks and issues, not captured lessons learned.

distractors.text: A single lessons learned register that is simply renamed as the repository when the project formally closes
distractors.trap: They are distinct: a project document versus an enterprise-level repository (organizational process asset).

explanation: The lessons learned register is created early and updated throughout the project. At closeout its entries are transferred into the organizational lessons learned repository so future projects can reuse them.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Trace requirements to objectives and tests
prompts: Midway through delivery a stakeholder asks whether a particular test actually maps back to an approved business objective, and whether any requirements have been left untested. Which single tool best answers both concerns?

correct.text: The requirements traceability matrix
correct.why: It links each requirement from its origin and business objective through design and test, exposing untested requirements and orphaned tests.

distractors.text: The requirements management plan
distractors.trap: It defines how requirements will be managed and processed but does not itself trace individual requirements to objectives and tests.

distractors.text: The scope baseline
distractors.trap: It defines approved scope (scope statement, WBS, WBS dictionary) but does not provide requirement-to-test traceability.

distractors.text: The product backlog
distractors.trap: It is an ordered list of work items and does not provide bidirectional traceability to objectives and tests.

explanation: The requirements traceability matrix maps requirements bidirectionally from business objectives through design, build, and test. It reveals both untested requirements and tests that trace to no approved objective.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Improve flow using Lean and Kanban
prompts: A Kanban team finds work piling up in the testing column while developers keep pulling new stories into development. The team wants a Lean mechanism that exposes the bottleneck and forces work to be finished before more is started. What should they apply?

correct.text: Set a work-in-progress (WIP) limit on the board columns
correct.why: Limiting WIP surfaces bottlenecks and pulls the team toward finishing started work before pulling in new work.

distractors.text: Increase batch size so more stories move through testing at once
distractors.trap: Larger batches worsen flow and hide bottlenecks, the opposite of Lean flow principles.

distractors.text: Move the unfinished testing work to the next iteration and keep developers starting new stories
distractors.trap: This ignores the bottleneck and increases work in progress, which is the root problem.

distractors.text: Remove the testing column from the board to simplify the flow
distractors.trap: This hides the problem rather than managing flow; the testing work still must be done.

explanation: WIP limits are the core Lean and Kanban mechanism for managing flow. By capping how much work can be in a column, they make bottlenecks visible and create pull pressure to finish before starting, easing the testing pile-up.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

prompts: Two senior stakeholders disagree about which requirements are mandatory for the first release versus merely desirable. The business analyst needs a transparent technique to classify each requirement by necessity. Which technique fits best?

correct.text: MoSCoW prioritization
correct.why: MoSCoW classifies each requirement as Must have, Should have, Could have, or Will not have this time, giving a transparent necessity ranking.

distractors.text: Decomposition into a WBS
distractors.trap: A WBS organizes scope and deliverables; it is not a requirements prioritization technique.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo simulation
distractors.trap: Monte Carlo is a quantitative risk and schedule analysis method, not a way to prioritize requirements.

distractors.text: A fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram
distractors.trap: A cause-and-effect diagram is a root-cause analysis tool, not a prioritization technique.

explanation: MoSCoW is a widely used requirements prioritization technique that sorts requirements into Must, Should, Could, and Will not have categories, making necessity decisions explicit and reducing stakeholder disputes.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Plan and manage the business case and financial selection
prompts: When comparing two approved candidate projects using standard financial selection criteria, which decision rules correctly favor the more financially attractive option? Select three.

correct.text: Prefer the project with the higher net present value
correct.why: A higher NPV means greater total value created in present-value terms, so it is preferred.

correct.text: Prefer the project with the higher internal rate of return
correct.why: A higher IRR indicates a greater return relative to the cost of capital, so it is preferred.

correct.text: Prefer the project with the shorter payback period
correct.why: A shorter payback recovers the investment faster, reducing exposure, so it is preferred when other factors are equal.

distractors.text: Prefer the project with the lower benefit-cost ratio
distractors.trap: A higher benefit-cost ratio is better; a BCR above 1 means benefits exceed costs, so lower is the wrong direction.

distractors.text: Prefer the project with the larger sunk cost already incurred
distractors.trap: Sunk costs are unrecoverable and must be excluded from forward-looking selection decisions entirely.

explanation: Standard business-case selection rules: choose higher NPV, higher IRR, BCR greater than 1 (and higher is better), and shorter payback. Lower BCR is the wrong direction, and sunk cost should never influence a go-forward selection.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Assess and manage risk using quantitative analysis
prompts: A team builds a decision tree for two design paths. Path A has a 60 percent chance of a 100k gain and a 40 percent chance of a 50k loss. Path B has a 50 percent chance of an 80k gain and a 50 percent chance of a 10k gain. Using expected monetary value to decide, which path should be selected?

correct.text: Path B, because its expected monetary value of 45k exceeds Path A at 40k
correct.why: Path B EMV = 0.5(80k) + 0.5(10k) = 45k, which is higher than Path A EMV = 0.6(100k) + 0.4(-50k) = 40k.

distractors.text: Path A, because it offers the single largest possible payoff of 100k
distractors.trap: EMV decisions weigh probability-adjusted outcomes, not the largest possible payoff in isolation.

distractors.text: Path A, because its expected monetary value of 60k is the highest
distractors.trap: This ignores the 40 percent chance of a 50k loss; the correct EMV for Path A is 40k, not 60k.

distractors.text: Path B, because it has no possibility of a loss regardless of expected value
distractors.trap: Avoiding any loss is risk preference, not the EMV rule; the EMV comparison, not loss avoidance, is what selects B here.

explanation: Expected monetary value multiplies each outcome by its probability and sums them. Path A = 0.6(100k) + 0.4(-50k) = 40k. Path B = 0.5(80k) + 0.5(10k) = 45k. The higher EMV is Path B, so the EMV decision rule selects Path B.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Plan and manage procurement and make-or-buy analysis
prompts: A project has already spent 200k building a component in-house. To finish it internally will cost another 150k, while buying an equivalent finished component from a vendor costs 120k. Using a proper make-or-buy analysis, what should the project manager recommend?

correct.text: Buy the component, because the remaining cost to make it (150k) exceeds the purchase cost (120k) and the 200k already spent is a sunk cost
correct.why: Make-or-buy compares only future costs; 150k to finish versus 120k to buy favors buying, and the 200k sunk cost is excluded.

distractors.text: Make the component, because the organization has already invested 200k that would be wasted if it switched to buying
distractors.trap: This is escalation of commitment; the 200k is unrecoverable and must not drive the forward decision.

distractors.text: Make the component, because the total make cost of 350k reflects the real investment and still creates more value than buying at 120k
distractors.trap: Adding the 200k sunk cost into the make total double-counts unrecoverable spend and distorts the comparison.

distractors.text: Buy the component, because outsourcing to a vendor generally costs less than continuing to build the part in-house
distractors.trap: Buy is not the right call for that reason; the decision rests on comparing remaining costs (150k vs 120k), not a general preference for vendors.

explanation: Make-or-buy analysis compares the relevant future costs of each option. Only the 150k to finish in-house versus the 120k to buy matters. The 200k already spent is a sunk cost and is excluded. Because 120k is less than 150k, buying is the better recommendation.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Select an appropriate delivery approach using sense-making models
prompts: Using the Cynefin framework, a project faces a problem where cause and effect are not knowable in advance and only become clear in hindsight, so the right path must emerge through experimentation. Which response is most appropriate?

correct.text: Probe, sense, and respond, running safe-to-fail experiments to let patterns emerge
correct.why: In the complex domain, cause and effect are only clear in retrospect, so probing with experiments to surface emergent practice is correct.

distractors.text: Sense, categorize, and respond by applying an established best practice
distractors.trap: Categorize and apply best practice is the clear (simple) domain response, where cause and effect are obvious.

distractors.text: Sense, analyze, and respond by bringing in experts to find the right answer
distractors.trap: Expert analysis is the complicated domain response, where good practice exists but requires analysis; complex problems have no knowable answer in advance.

distractors.text: Act, sense, and respond by taking immediate action to establish order
distractors.trap: Immediate action to establish order is the chaotic domain response, used when there is no time to probe.

explanation: Cynefin matches problem domains to responses. Clear: sense-categorize-respond with best practice. Complicated: sense-analyze-respond with expert good practice. Complex: probe-sense-respond with emergent experiments. Chaotic: act-sense-respond to establish order. Cause and effect knowable only in hindsight describes the complex domain, so probe-sense-respond is correct.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Select an appropriate delivery approach using sense-making models
prompts: A critical production system has just failed during a live event and there is no time to analyze options or run experiments. Under the Cynefin framework, which domain is this and what is the correct first move?

correct.text: Chaotic domain: act first to stabilize, then sense and respond
correct.why: In the chaotic domain there is no time to gather data, so the leader acts immediately to establish order before sensing and responding.

distractors.text: Complex domain: probe with small experiments before acting
distractors.trap: Probing takes time the crisis does not allow; experimentation fits the complex domain, not an urgent collapse.

distractors.text: Complicated domain: pause and bring in experts to analyze the failure
distractors.trap: Analysis by experts fits the complicated domain; in chaos there is no time to analyze before acting.

distractors.text: Clear domain: apply the standard documented best-practice procedure
distractors.trap: A clear-domain best practice assumes a stable, well-understood situation, which an unfolding crisis is not.

explanation: A live, urgent collapse with no time to analyze is the chaotic Cynefin domain. The correct sequence is act-sense-respond: take immediate action to establish stability, then sense the new situation and respond to move toward order. Probing, analyzing, or applying routine best practice all assume time or stability that does not exist.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Recognize and counter cognitive biases in estimating
prompts: During estimation, a stakeholder opens by stating the project should cost about 500k. Even after the team gathers detailed bottom-up data, every revised estimate clusters tightly around that first 500k figure. Which cognitive bias is most likely at work?

correct.text: Anchoring bias, because the first number mentioned sets a reference point that pulls every later estimate toward it despite new data
correct.why: Anchoring is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information, pulling later judgments toward it.

distractors.text: Sunk-cost fallacy, because prior unrecoverable spending on the work is dictating how the revised estimates come out
distractors.trap: No money has been spent yet; sunk cost concerns unrecoverable past expenditure, not an initial reference number.

distractors.text: Groupthink, because the team is suppressing disagreement and converging on a single figure to preserve harmony
distractors.trap: Groupthink is suppression of dissent for harmony; the issue here is fixation on a starting number, not conformity pressure.

distractors.text: Availability bias, because vivid and easily recalled recent events are shaping how the team sets the estimate
distractors.trap: Availability bias relies on easily recalled examples; the distortion here stems from a stated opening figure, not recall ease.

explanation: Anchoring bias occurs when an initial value disproportionately influences later judgments. Estimates clustering around the first 500k figure despite fresh bottom-up data signal anchoring. Countermeasures include estimating independently before sharing numbers and using ranges. Sunk cost, groupthink, and availability describe different biases.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process

eco_task: Recover and stabilize a troubled project
prompts: A project manager inherits a troubled project: morale is low, the schedule and budget are unreliable, and stakeholders distrust the status reports. What should the project manager do first to begin recovery?

correct.text: Stabilize the situation and run an honest, independent assessment of true scope, schedule, cost, and risks before committing to a recovery plan
correct.why: Recovery starts with stabilizing and establishing the real baseline so decisions and a credible reset rest on facts, not optimistic reports.

distractors.text: Immediately recommit to the original deadline and authorize overtime and weekend work to claw back the lost schedule
distractors.trap: Committing to the old deadline before assessing reality repeats the optimism that caused the trouble and ignores root causes.

distractors.text: Recommend cancelling the project to the sponsor because the unreliable baselines make a successful recovery unlikely
distractors.trap: A kill decision may be valid, but it should follow an honest assessment at a defined gate, not precede any analysis.

distractors.text: Replace the entire project team to signal a fresh start and restore stakeholder confidence before reviewing the facts
distractors.trap: Wholesale replacement destroys knowledge and trust without addressing the actual scope, schedule, and risk problems.

explanation: Recovering a troubled project begins with stabilizing operations and conducting an honest, independent assessment to expose the true state of scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk. Only with a credible baseline can the project manager propose an honest reset, and only at a defined decision gate should a kill or continue choice be made. Jumping to deadlines, cancellation, or team changes before the assessment is premature.
references: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition
PMP Examination Content Outline 2026 - Process
